items.explanation: 部 (bù) is the classifier for movies, novels, phones, and machines. 一部电影 means 'one movie.' While 个 could work informally, using 部 is more precise and natural. You'll frequently encounter: 一部手机 (a cellphone), 一部小说 (a novel), 一部电脑 (a computer). The character 部 suggests a complex, assembled whole — fitting for films, devices, and literary works.

items.question: 三_____课
items.explanation: 节 (jié) is the classifier for class periods, lessons, and segments. 三节课 means 'three class periods.' In Chinese schools and universities, class schedules are organized by 节: 第一节课 (first period class). Note that 门 (mén) is used for courses or subjects as a whole (一门课 = one course/subject), while 节 refers to individual lesson sessions. This distinction matters in academic contexts.

items.question: 一_____树
items.explanation: 棵 (kē) is the classifier for trees, plants, and vegetables that grow from the ground. 一棵树 means 'one tree.' The distinction between plant classifiers is logical: 棵 for whole plants rooted in earth, 朵 for flowers/clouds, 根 for long thin objects like sticks or carrots. Examples: 一棵草 (a blade/stalk of grass), 两棵白菜 (two cabbages). This classifier connects to the natural world you'll describe in Chinese.

items.question: 一_____饭
items.explanation: 碗 (wǎn) means 'bowl' and is the classifier for food served in bowls. 一碗饭 means 'a bowl of rice.' In Chinese dining culture, rice and noodles are typically served in bowls, making this classifier essential at mealtimes. Compare: 一碗面 (a bowl of noodles), 一碗汤 (a bowl of soup). For food on plates, use 盘 (pán): 一盘菜 (a plate of dishes). Mastering food classifiers makes ordering meals much smoother.

items.question: 一_____刀
items.explanation: 把 (bǎ) is the classifier for objects with handles or that you grip — knives, chairs, umbrellas, keys, scissors, and fans. 一把刀 means 'one knife.' The connection is intuitive: 把 relates to the hand radical 扌and gripping. Examples: 一把椅子 (a chair), 一把伞 (an umbrella), 一把钥匙 (a key). Note that 把 also serves as a very important grammatical particle (the 把 construction), which you'll learn separately.

name: Modal Verbs
description: Expressing ability, permission, and necessity in Chinese

items.question: 我_____说中文
items.explanation: 会 (huì) expresses learned ability — skills acquired through study or practice. 我会说中文 means 'I can speak Chinese' (because I've learned it). The distinction between 会, 能, and 可以 is one of the trickiest aspects of Chinese for English speakers, since English uses just 'can.' Remember: 会 = learned skill. You'll say 会游泳 (can swim), 会做饭 (can cook), 会开车 (can drive) — all acquired abilities.

items.question: 你_____帮我吗？
items.explanation: 能 (néng) expresses physical ability or circumstantial possibility — whether conditions allow something. 你能帮我吗 means 'Can you help me?' (Are you able to / Is it possible for you?). Unlike 会 (learned skill), 能 asks about capability given the circumstances. Compare: 我会游泳 (I can swim — I know how) vs. 今天太冷，我不能游泳 (It's too cold today, I can't swim — conditions don't allow it).

items.question: 这里_____抽烟吗？
items.explanation: 可以 (kěyǐ) expresses permission — whether something is allowed or acceptable. 这里可以抽烟吗 means 'Is smoking allowed here?' While 能 can also express permission informally, 可以 is the standard choice when asking about rules and regulations. The negative form 不可以 means 'not allowed/may not.' In polite requests, 可以 sounds courteous: 我可以坐这里吗？(May I sit here?)

items.question: 我_____去中国
items.explanation: 想 (xiǎng) expresses desire or intention — 'want to' or 'would like to.' 我想去中国 means 'I want to go to China.' This is one of the most useful modal verbs for expressing your wishes. Unlike the more formal 想要 (xiǎngyào), plain 想 sounds natural in conversation. It can also mean 'to think' or 'to miss': 我想你 (I miss you), 我想想 (let me think about it). Context determines the meaning.

items.question: 明天我_____上班
items.translation: (Tomorrow I have to go to work)
items.explanation: 要 (yào) expresses necessity, strong intention, or future plans — 'need to,' 'must,' or 'going to.' 明天我要上班 means 'Tomorrow I have to go to work.' 要 is multifunctional: it can express want (我要一杯水 = I want a glass of water), must (你要小心 = you must be careful), or future (要下雨了 = it's going to rain). The negative is tricky: 不要 means 'don't' (command), not 'don't need to.'

items.question: 你_____多喝水
items.explanation: 应该 (yīnggāi) means 'should' or 'ought to' — giving advice or expressing expectation. 你应该多喝水 means 'You should drink more water.' This is essential for giving and receiving advice in Chinese. It's softer than 必须 (must) but stronger than a suggestion. Common patterns: 你应该休息 (you should rest), 我们应该早点走 (we should leave earlier). The shortened form 该 is also common in speech.

items.question: 学生_____做作业
items.explanation: 必须 (bìxū) expresses strong obligation — 'must' or 'have to' with no room for negotiation. 学生必须做作业 means 'Students must do homework.' It's stronger than 应该 (should) and 要 (need to). Use 必须 for rules, strict requirements, and unwavering necessities. The negative form is special: instead of 不必须, Chinese uses 不必 (bùbì) or 不用 (bùyòng) meaning 'need not.' This asymmetry is important to remember.

items.question: 你不_____担心
items.explanation: 不用 (bùyòng) means 'needn't' or 'don't need to' — expressing that something is unnecessary. 你不用担心 means 'You don't need to worry.' This is the standard way to negate necessity in Chinese. Note that while 必须 means 'must,' its negation is NOT 不必须 but rather 不用 or 不必. Common reassurances: 不用谢 (you're welcome / no need to thank), 不用客气 (no need to be polite). This phrase is incredibly useful socially.

items.question: 我_____试试这个菜
items.explanation: 想要 (xiǎngyào) is a slightly more emphatic or formal version of 想, meaning 'would like to' or 'want to.' 我想要试试这个菜 means 'I'd like to try this dish.' While 想 alone is sufficient in casual speech, 想要 adds a touch of politeness or emphasis that's appropriate in restaurants and formal settings. It also combines two useful words: 想 (want/think) + 要 (want/need), reinforcing both concepts.

items.question: 我小时候不_____游泳
items.translation: (When I was little, I couldn't swim)
items.explanation: 不会 (bù huì) negates learned abilities — 'cannot' because one hasn't acquired the skill. 我小时候不会游泳 means 'When I was little, I couldn't swim' (hadn't learned yet). This contrasts with 不能 (circumstances prevent it) and 不可以 (not permitted). The past context here makes it clear: as a child, you hadn't yet learned the skill. Today you might say 我现在会了 (now I can) — showing the skill has been acquired.

items.question: 你_____不要迟到
items.explanation: 千万 (qiānwàn) literally means 'ten million' but functions as an emphatic adverb meaning 'absolutely must' or 'by all means.' 你千万不要迟到 means 'You absolutely must not be late.' This intensifier adds urgency to commands and warnings. It pairs with 不要 (don't) or 要 (must): 千万不要忘记 (absolutely don't forget), 千万要小心 (you absolutely must be careful). It conveys the speaker's deep concern.

items.question: 他_____来，也可能不来
items.translation: (He may come, or may not come)
items.explanation: 可能 (kěnéng) means 'possibly' or 'may' — expressing uncertainty about whether something will happen. 他可能来 means 'He may come.' Unlike modal verbs that express ability or permission, 可能 expresses probability. It's incredibly versatile: 可能会下雨 (it might rain), 这可能是真的 (this might be true). 可能 can function as an adverb, adjective, or noun (可能性 = possibility), making it one of the most flexible words in Chinese.

items.question: 明天_____会很热
items.explanation: 一定 (yīdìng) means 'definitely,' 'certainly,' or 'must' (expressing strong prediction). 明天一定会很热 means 'Tomorrow it will definitely be hot.' While 必须 expresses obligation, 一定 expresses certainty or determination. Promises use 一定: 我一定来 (I'll definitely come). Predictions use 一定: 他一定很累 (he must be very tired). This word conveys confidence and conviction in your statements.

items.question: 你_____注意安全
items.explanation: 得 (děi) when pronounced with the third tone means 'must' or 'have to' in spoken Chinese. 你得注意安全 means 'You must pay attention to safety.' This colloquial modal verb is extremely common in daily speech and often preferred over the more formal 必须. Don't confuse it with 得 (de) as a complement particle or 得 (dé) meaning 'to obtain.' The three pronunciations of 得 represent three different words — context and tone tell them apart.

items.question: 我们_____走了
items.explanation: 该 (gāi) means 'should' or 'ought to' and is the colloquial shortened form of 应该. 我们该走了 means 'We should go now.' Adding 了 signals a change of state — it's now time to leave. 该 is used more in spoken Chinese than the full 应该: 该吃饭了 (it's time to eat), 该睡觉了 (it's time to sleep). It often implies that the appropriate moment has arrived, making it a natural way to suggest transitions in daily routines.

name: Location Expressions
description: Expressing locations, directions, and spatial relationships

items.question: 猫在桌子_____
items.explanation: 上面 (shàngmiàn) means 'on top of' or 'above.' 猫在桌子上面 means 'The cat is on the table.' Chinese location expressions follow a consistent pattern: Reference Object + Location Word. The shorter form 上 can replace 上面 in casual speech: 桌子上. This pattern is the backbone of all spatial descriptions in Chinese. Master the basic set — 上/下/里/外/前/后/左/右/旁 — and you can describe any position.

items.question: 书在书包_____
items.explanation: 里面 (lǐmiàn) means 'inside.' 书在书包里面 means 'The book is inside the bag.' This location word is essential for describing things within containers, buildings, and enclosed spaces. The pattern here is: Object + 在 + Container + 里面. Examples: 人在房间里面 (the person is in the room), 钱在口袋里面 (the money is in the pocket). You can shorten to just 里: 书包里.

items.question: 银行在学校_____
items.translation: (The bank is next to the school)
items.explanation: 旁边 (pángbiān) means 'beside' or 'next to.' 银行在学校旁边 means 'The bank is next to the school.' This is one of the most practical location words for giving and understanding directions. Unlike English's flexible 'next to,' 旁边 always follows the reference point. Related words: 附近 (fùjìn, nearby), 隔壁 (gébì, next door — specifically for adjacent rooms or buildings).

items.question: 超市在医院_____
items.translation: (The supermarket is across from the hospital)
items.explanation: 对面 (duìmiàn) means 'opposite' or 'across from.' 超市在医院对面 means 'The supermarket is across from the hospital.' This word is crucial for navigating Chinese cities and giving directions. You'll hear it constantly: 马路对面 (across the street), 对面的楼 (the building opposite). It can also mean 'face to face': 我们坐对面 (we sit facing each other). Learning this word dramatically improves your ability to understand directions.

items.question: 公园在学校_____
items.explanation: 后面 (hòumiàn) means 'behind' or 'at the back.' 公园在学校后面 means 'The park is behind the school.' The pair 前面/后面 (front/back) is fundamental to spatial descriptions. In Chinese culture, 前 often implies 'forward/future' and 后 implies 'backward/past' — 前天 (the day before yesterday, literally 'front day') and 后天 (the day after tomorrow, literally 'back day'). These temporal extensions make the words doubly useful.

items.question: 桌子_____有一只猫
items.translation: (There is a cat under the table)
items.explanation: 下面 (xiàmiàn) means 'below' or 'under.' 桌子下面有一只猫 means 'There is a cat under the table.' Note the different sentence pattern here: Location + 有 + Object (there is something at a location). This is the existential 有 pattern, which always puts location first. Compare: 猫在桌子下面 (The cat is under the table) vs. 桌子下面有一只猫 (Under the table there is a cat). Both are correct but emphasize different things.

items.question: 请在门_____等我
items.translation: (Please wait for me outside the door)
items.explanation: 外面 (wàimiàn) means 'outside.' 请在门外面等我 means 'Please wait for me outside the door.' The pair 里面/外面 (inside/outside) is among the most commonly used location words. 外面 can stand alone as a noun meaning 'outside, outdoors': 外面很冷 (It's cold outside), 我们去外面吃 (Let's eat outside). The shortened form 外 works in compounds: 门外 (outside the door), 国外 (abroad).

items.question: 邮局在超市_____
items.translation: (The post office is to the left of the supermarket)
items.explanation: 左边 (zuǒbiān) means 'on the left side.' 邮局在超市左边 means 'The post office is to the left of the supermarket.' The pair 左边/右边 (left/right) follows the same pattern as other location words. A helpful mnemonic: 左 (left) has a 工 component suggesting 'work' with your left hand, while 右 (right) has a 口 component suggesting you 'eat' (口=mouth) with your right hand. Directions in Chinese consistently use this Reference + Side pattern.

items.question: 图书馆_____有一个咖啡馆
items.translation: (There is a café in front of the library)
items.explanation: 前面 (qiánmiàn) means 'in front of' or 'ahead.' 图书馆前面有一个咖啡馆 means 'In front of the library there is a café.' Again using the Location + 有 + Object pattern for existence. 前面 is versatile: it describes spatial position (楼前面 = in front of the building), temporal future (前面的路 = the road ahead), and sequential position (前面两个人 = the two people in front). This multi-use nature makes it extremely common.

items.question: 他家在_____
items.explanation: 附近 (fùjìn) means 'nearby' or 'in the vicinity.' 他家在附近 means 'His home is nearby.' Unlike the more specific location words (上/下/左/右), 附近 gives a general sense of proximity without specifying exact position. It's invaluable for practical conversations: 附近有没有银行？(Is there a bank nearby?), 这附近 (around here). For travelers and residents alike, 附近 is a word you'll use daily.

items.question: 请坐_____
items.explanation: 这里 (zhèlǐ) means 'here.' 请坐这里 means 'Please sit here.' The trio 这里/那里/哪里 (here/there/where) forms a fundamental set. 这 (this/here) points to something close, 那 (that/there) points to something distant, and 哪 (which/where) asks a question. Note the alternate forms: 这儿/那儿/哪儿 are more common in northern Chinese (especially Beijing dialect), while 这里/那里/哪里 are more standard and southern.

items.question: 你的手机在_____？
items.explanation: 哪里 (nǎlǐ) means 'where.' 你的手机在哪里 means 'Where is your phone?' This question word is essential for navigation and finding things. Unlike English, Chinese doesn't change word order for questions — 哪里 sits exactly where the answer would go. Compare: 手机在桌子上 (The phone is on the table) → 手机在哪里？(Where is the phone?). Also, 哪里哪里 is a humble response to compliments, meaning 'not at all.'

items.question: 花园在房子_____和_____之间
items.translation: (The garden is between the front and back of the house)
items.explanation: 之间 (zhījiān) means 'between.' When combined with two reference points, it creates 'between A and B': A和B之间. This sentence shows how location words combine with 之间 for precise spatial descriptions. The pattern is flexible: 学校和医院之间 (between the school and hospital), 周一和周五之间 (between Monday and Friday). Note how 之间 extends from spatial to temporal contexts seamlessly.

items.question: 从这里_____走
items.explanation: 往前 (wǎng qián) means 'forward' or 'straight ahead.' 从这里往前走 means 'Go straight from here.' The preposition 往 (wǎng) indicates direction of movement and always precedes the direction word. Essential navigation phrases: 往前走 (go forward), 往左拐 (turn left), 往右拐 (turn right), 往回走 (go back). Combined with 从 (from), you can give complete directions: 从路口往右拐 (turn right at the intersection).

items.question: 学校_____邮局很近
items.translation: (The school is very close to the post office)
items.explanation: 离 (lí) expresses distance between two points — 'from' in terms of how far apart things are. 学校离邮局很近 means 'The school is very close to the post office.' The pattern is: A + 离 + B + 距离描述. Examples: 我家离公司很远 (My home is far from the office), 这里离机场不远 (It's not far from the airport to here). Don't confuse 离 (distance) with 从 (starting point) — they serve different spatial functions.

name: Conjunctions (Basic)
description: Connecting ideas with basic Chinese conjunctions

items.question: 我_____他都喜欢音乐
items.explanation: 和 (hé) means 'and' and connects nouns and noun phrases. 我和他都喜欢音乐 means 'Both he and I like music.' An important distinction: unlike English 'and,' 和 generally connects only nouns, not clauses or verbs. You wouldn't say 我吃饭和看电视 (I eat and watch TV) — instead, you'd list actions without a conjunction or use other connectors. This noun-only rule is one of the most common mistakes English speakers make in Chinese.

items.question: 你想喝茶_____咖啡？
items.explanation: 还是 (háishì) means 'or' in questions where you're choosing between options. 你想喝茶还是咖啡 means 'Do you want tea or coffee?' This is different from 或者 (huòzhě), which means 'or' in statements. The distinction is crucial: 还是 for questions (tea OR coffee?), 或者 for statements (we can have tea or coffee). This question-or vs. statement-or split doesn't exist in English, making it a uniquely Chinese grammar point to master.

items.question: 你可以坐公交车_____地铁去
items.translation: (You can go by bus or subway)
items.explanation: 或者 (huòzhě) means 'or' in statements (not questions). 你可以坐公交车或者地铁去 means 'You can go by bus or subway.' In this statement listing options, 或者 is correct because you're describing possibilities, not asking someone to choose. Compare: 你坐公交车还是地铁？(question — which do you pick?) vs. 你可以坐公交车或者地铁 (statement — both are options). Mastering this 还是/或者 distinction marks a real step in your Chinese.

items.question: 他很聪明，_____不努力
items.translation: (He is smart, but doesn't work hard)
items.explanation: 但是 (dànshì) means 'but' or 'however' — introducing a contrast. 他很聪明但是不努力 means 'He is smart but doesn't work hard.' Unlike English, Chinese often pairs 虽然 (although) with 但是: 虽然他很聪明，但是不努力 (Although he's smart, he doesn't work hard). However, 但是 can stand alone without 虽然. Shortened forms include 但 and 可是 (kěshì), which is slightly more colloquial.

items.question: _____下雨了，_____我没去
items.explanation: 因为...所以 (yīnwèi...suǒyǐ) is the classic cause-and-effect pair meaning 'because...therefore.' 因为下雨了，所以我没去 means 'Because it rained, (therefore) I didn't go.' Unlike English, Chinese often uses both halves of the pair together. However, in casual speech, you can use just one: 因为下雨了，我没去 or 下雨了，所以我没去. This paired conjunction pattern is very characteristic of Chinese grammar.

items.question: _____你不舒服，_____在家休息吧
items.translation: (If you're not comfortable, then rest at home)
items.explanation: 如果...就 (rúguǒ...jiù) means 'if...then' — expressing conditions. 如果你不舒服，就在家休息吧 means 'If you're not feeling well, then rest at home.' Chinese conditional sentences pair 如果 with 就 (then), though either half can be omitted in casual speech. Unlike English, there's no complex tense shifting for conditionals — Chinese relies on context and particles rather than verb forms to show hypothetical situations.

items.question: _____她学习好，_____很有礼貌
items.translation: (Not only does she study well, but she's also very polite)
items.explanation: 不但...而且 (bùdàn...érqiě) means 'not only...but also' — adding emphasis by listing positive qualities or facts. 不但她学习好，而且很有礼貌 means 'Not only does she study well, but she's also very polite.' This is one of Chinese's most elegant conjunction pairs. It shows that something exceeds expectations. Note: when the subject is the same, 不但 comes after it; when subjects differ, 不但 comes before the first subject.

items.question: 我先吃饭，_____去上班
items.translation: (I eat first, then go to work)
items.explanation: 然后 (ránhòu) means 'then' or 'after that' — connecting sequential actions. 我先吃饭，然后去上班 means 'I eat first, then go to work.' The pair 先...然后 (first...then) is the standard way to describe the order of actions. Other sequence words: 接着 (jiēzhe, next/following that), 最后 (zuìhòu, finally). Chinese expresses temporal sequence explicitly with these connectors, making narratives clear and logical.

items.question: _____他很忙，_____他还来帮我
items.translation: (Although he is busy, he still came to help me)
items.explanation: 虽然...但是 (suīrán...dànshì) means 'although...but' — expressing concession. 虽然他很忙，但是他还来帮我 means 'Although he is busy, he still came to help me.' Note that Chinese uses BOTH 'although' AND 'but' together, while English uses only one. This double marking of contrast is a distinctive feature of Chinese grammar. You can add 还 (still) or 却 (yet) to the second clause for emphasis.

items.question: 我_____想学中文_____想学日文
items.translation: (I both want to learn Chinese and want to learn Japanese)
items.explanation: 既...又 (jì...yòu) means 'both...and' — expressing that two qualities or states coexist. 我既想学中文又想学日文 means 'I both want to learn Chinese and want to learn Japanese.' This conjunction emphasizes that two things are simultaneously true. Compare with 又...又 which is similar but slightly less formal. Example: 这个地方既安静又方便 (This place is both quiet and convenient). It's a powerful way to express dual qualities.

items.question: 我一边听音乐_____做作业
items.translation: (I listen to music while doing homework)
items.explanation: 一边...一边 (yībiān...yībiān) means 'while...at the same time' — expressing simultaneous actions. 我一边听音乐一边做作业 means 'I listen to music while doing homework.' Both actions happen together. This differs from 先...然后 (sequential) and emphasizes simultaneity. Common examples: 一边走一边聊 (walk and chat at the same time), 一边吃一边看电视 (eat while watching TV). The pattern requires both 一边s.

items.question: 只要你努力，_____能成功
items.translation: (As long as you work hard, you can succeed)
items.explanation: 只要...就 (zhǐyào...jiù) means 'as long as...then' — stating a sufficient condition. 只要你努力，就能成功 means 'As long as you work hard, you can succeed.' Compare with 只有...才 (only if...then), which states a necessary condition. The difference matters: 只要 says the condition is enough; 只有 says it's required. 只要你来，我就高兴 (As long as you come, I'll be happy) — your coming alone is sufficient for my happiness.

items.question: 她不喜欢唱歌，_____不喜欢跳舞
items.translation: (She doesn't like singing, nor does she like dancing)
items.explanation: 也 (yě) means 'also' or 'nor' (in negative sentences). 她不喜欢唱歌，也不喜欢跳舞 means 'She doesn't like singing, nor does she like dancing.' In positive sentences, 也 means 'also': 我也喜欢 (I also like it). In negative sentences, 也不 functions like 'nor' or 'neither': 我也不知道 (I don't know either). 也 always comes before the verb, never at the end. This small word is one of the most frequently used in all of Chinese.

items.question: 不管天气怎么样，我_____去跑步
items.translation: (No matter what the weather is like, I will still go running)
items.explanation: 不管...都 (bùguǎn...dōu) means 'no matter...all/still' — expressing that the result is unchanged regardless of conditions. 不管天气怎么样，我都去跑步 means 'No matter what the weather, I'll still go running.' 不管 can pair with 都 or 也 in the second clause. Similar patterns: 无论...都 (more formal), 不论...都. Examples: 不管你去不去，我都去 (Whether you go or not, I'm going). This pattern shows determination and unconditional commitment.

items.question: 你来_____不来？
items.explanation: The A还是不A pattern (verb + 还是 + 不 + verb) is a direct way to ask yes-or-no questions in Chinese. 你来还是不来 means 'Are you coming or not?' This is more direct than adding 吗 and demands a clear answer. Similar to the V不V pattern (来不来？) but slightly more emphatic. Other examples: 你去还是不去？(Are you going or not?), 你同意还是不同意？(Do you agree or not?). This pattern is useful when clarity is needed.

name: Possessives
description: Expressing ownership and possession in Chinese

items.question: 这是_____手机
items.explanation: 我的 (wǒ de) means 'my' — formed simply by adding 的 (de) to the pronoun 我 (I/me). 这是我的手机 means 'This is my phone.' Chinese possessives are beautifully systematic: just add 的 to any pronoun. 我的 (my), 你的 (your), 他的 (his), 她的 (her), 我们的 (our), 你们的 (your, plural), 他们的 (their). No irregular forms, no case changes. This simplicity is one of the genuine delights of Chinese grammar.

items.question: 那是_____老师
items.explanation: 我们的 (wǒmen de) means 'our.' 那是我们的老师 means 'That is our teacher.' The plural pronouns follow the same possessive pattern: pronoun + 的. Note that 我们 (we/us) becomes 我们的 (our), 你们 (you all) becomes 你们的 (your, plural), 他们 (they) becomes 他们的 (their). The suffix 们 (men) pluralizes pronouns, and 的 makes them possessive — two simple rules that unlock all possessive forms.

items.question: 这本书是_____
items.explanation: When the possessed noun is clear from context, 的 can stand alone without repeating the noun: 这本书是你的 means 'This book is yours' (literally 'this book is your-的'). The 的 absorbs the noun. This is exactly like English 'yours/mine/hers' vs. 'your/my/her.' Compare: 这件衣服是你的还是我的？(Is this clothing yours or mine?). The pattern Noun + 是 + Pronoun + 的 elegantly states ownership.

items.question: 他是我_____
items.explanation: With close relationships (family members, close friends), the possessive 的 is often dropped. 他是我爸爸 means 'He is my dad' — no 的 needed between 我 and 爸爸. This rule applies to intimate relationships: 我妈妈 (my mom), 我哥哥 (my older brother), 我朋友 (my friend), 我老师 (my teacher). Dropping 的 signals closeness and familiarity. Adding 的 (我的爸爸) sounds slightly more distant or formal, like saying 'a father of mine.'

items.question: _____是谁的？
items.explanation: 谁的 (shéi de) means 'whose.' 这是谁的 means 'Whose is this?' The question word 谁 (who) + 的 creates the possessive question form, just as any pronoun + 的 creates possessives. This follows the beautiful consistency of Chinese: the same pattern works for questions and answers. 这是谁的书？(Whose book is this?) → 这是我的书 (This is my book). The question word sits where the answer goes — no word order change needed.

items.question: 小李的_____很漂亮
items.explanation: Names and titles also use the Possessor + 的 + Noun pattern. 小李的房子很漂亮 means 'Xiao Li's house is very beautiful.' This works identically to English's apostrophe-s: 老师的书 (the teacher's book), 中国的文化 (China's culture), 公司的员工 (the company's employees). The 的 is the universal possessive connector, working for people, places, organizations, and abstract concepts alike.

items.question: 这个学校_____学生很多
items.translation: (This school's students are many / This school has many students)
items.explanation: 的 (de) can link any modifier to a noun, extending beyond simple possession. 这个学校的学生很多 means 'This school's students are numerous.' Here, 的 shows that the students belong to the school — institutional rather than personal possession. This broader use of 的 covers: 今天的天气 (today's weather), 北京的冬天 (Beijing's winter), 你说的话 (the words you said). Understanding 的 as a general modifier marker, not just possession, is key.

items.question: 她_____猫很可爱
items.explanation: 她的 (tā de) means 'her.' 她的猫很可爱 means 'Her cat is very cute.' An interesting point: in spoken Chinese, 他 (he), 她 (she), and 它 (it) all sound exactly the same — 'tā.' The possessive forms 他的/她的/它的 are also identical in speech. Only the written characters distinguish gender. This means context is crucial in spoken Chinese. In writing, the distinction is clear, but in conversation, you rely on context to determine the referent.

items.question: 那辆红色_____车是我的
items.explanation: When adjectives modify nouns, 的 often appears between them, especially for longer or more descriptive phrases. 那辆红色的车是我的 means 'That red car is mine.' Note the double use of 的: 红色的 (red — adjective + 的) and 我的 (mine — possessive + 的). For single-syllable adjectives, 的 is often omitted: 红车 (red car). For multi-syllable adjectives, 的 is generally required: 漂亮的花 (beautiful flowers), 便宜的东西 (cheap things).

items.question: 这个_____你的吗？
items.explanation: To ask about ownership, use 是...的吗 pattern. 这个是你的吗 means 'Is this one yours?' The answer can be: 是，是我的 (Yes, it's mine) or 不是，不是我的 (No, it's not mine). This question pattern is extremely practical — you'll use it in lost-and-found situations, at restaurants, in shared spaces, and anywhere ownership needs clarification. It combines the 是 (is) structure with the possessive 的 naturally.

items.question: 桌子上的书是_____
items.translation: (The book on the table is his)
items.explanation: 他的 (tā de) means 'his.' 桌子上的书是他的 means 'The book on the table is his.' This sentence shows a complex use of 的: 桌子上的书 (the book on the table — 的 links 桌子上 as a modifier to 书) and then 是他的 (is his — possessive 的). The ability of 的 to create these nested modifications is a powerful feature of Chinese grammar, allowing you to build descriptive phrases without relative pronouns or complex clauses.

items.question: 你有_____手机号码吗？
items.explanation: 她的 (tā de) means 'her.' 你有她的手机号码吗 means 'Do you have her phone number?' This sentence combines the verb 有 (have) with a possessive phrase. Notice how naturally possessives work in Chinese — no case changes, no gender agreement issues. The possessive simply slots in: 你有 + 她的手机号码 + 吗. Whether asking about 我的 (my), 你的 (your), or 她的 (her) things, the structure remains perfectly consistent.

items.question: 我们公司_____产品很好
items.explanation: Possessives extend to organizations and groups: 我们公司的产品 means 'our company's products.' Note how Chinese chains possessives: 我们 + 公司 + 的 + 产品. The 的 connects the company to its products. You can build longer chains: 我们公司的经理的办公室 (our company's manager's office). In practice, overly long chains are avoided for clarity, but the mechanism shows how 的 can link any possessor to any possessed item.

items.question: _____想法很好
items.explanation: 你的 (nǐ de) means 'your.' 你的想法很好 means 'Your idea is very good.' Note that for abstract nouns like 想法 (idea/thought), 的 is typically kept — unlike family members where it can be dropped. Compare: 我想法 (sounds incomplete) vs. 我的想法 (my idea — correct). The rule of thumb: drop 的 only with close personal relationships (family, close friends, institutions you belong to). For abstract concepts and objects, keep 的.

items.question: 这些都是_____
items.explanation: When claiming ownership for a group, 我们的 (ours) works independently as a pronoun. 这些都是我们的 means 'These are all ours.' The word 都 (dōu, all) adds emphasis — everything mentioned belongs to us. This possessive pronoun usage mirrors English: 这些是我们的 (these are ours), 那些是他们的 (those are theirs). Using possessive pronouns independently gives your Chinese a natural, fluent quality that avoids unnecessary repetition of nouns.

name: Demonstratives
description: Using 这/那 (this/that) and their variants for pointing and referencing

items.question: _____是我的书
items.explanation: 这 (zhè) means 'this' and points to something nearby. 这是我的书 means 'This is my book.' In Chinese, 这 functions as both a demonstrative pronoun and determiner. When used alone before 是, it acts as a pronoun meaning 'this one.' The tone is fourth tone (falling), making it sound decisive and clear. Compare with 那 (nà, that) for distant objects.

items.question: _____个人是谁？
items.explanation: 那 (nà) means 'that' and refers to something farther away. 那个人是谁？means 'Who is that person?' When 那 is followed by a measure word like 个, it becomes a determiner: 那个 (that one). This is one of the most common constructions in daily Chinese — you'll hear 那个 constantly in conversation.

items.question: 你要_____杯咖啡？
items.translation: (Which cup of coffee do you want?)
items.explanation: 哪 (nǎ) is the interrogative demonstrative meaning 'which.' 你要哪杯咖啡？asks 'Which cup of coffee do you want?' Notice 哪 has a third tone (rising-dipping) while 那 has a fourth tone (falling). This tonal difference is crucial — mixing them up changes a question into a statement. Always pair 哪 with a measure word.

items.question: _____些苹果很甜
items.explanation: 这些 (zhèxiē) means 'these' — the plural form of 这. 这些苹果很甜 means 'These apples are very sweet.' The character 些 (xiē) transforms singular demonstratives into plurals: 这些 (these), 那些 (those), 哪些 (which ones). This is one of the few ways Chinese explicitly marks plurality.

items.question: _____些学生都很聪明
items.explanation: 那些 (nàxiē) means 'those' — the plural of 那. 那些学生都很聪明 means 'Those students are all very smart.' Notice 都 (dōu, all) often appears with 那些 to emphasize that every member of the group shares the quality. The combination 那些...都 is extremely natural in Chinese.

items.question: 你想买_____本书？
items.translation: (Which book do you want to buy?)
items.explanation: 哪本书 uses 哪 (nǎ, which) with the measure word 本 (běn, for books). 你想买哪本书？means 'Which book do you want to buy?' Each noun in Chinese has a specific measure word, and 哪 must pair with the correct one. For books it's 本, for people it's 个, for flat objects it's 张. Mastering these pairings is essential.

items.question: _____里是图书馆
items.explanation: 这里 (zhèlǐ) means 'here' — literally 'this place.' 这里是图书馆 means 'Here is the library.' Chinese builds location words by combining demonstratives with 里 (inside/place): 这里 (here), 那里 (there), 哪里 (where). You'll also hear 这儿/那儿/哪儿 in northern dialects — same meaning, slightly more casual.

items.question: 你家在_____里？
items.explanation: 哪里 (nǎlǐ) means 'where' — the interrogative location word. 你家在哪里？means 'Where is your home?' This is one of the first questions you'll learn and use constantly. Fun fact: 哪里哪里 (nǎlǐ nǎlǐ) repeated is a humble response to compliments, literally 'where where' but meaning 'you flatter me.'

items.question: 请把_____个给我
items.explanation: 那个 (nàge) is probably the most frequently used demonstrative phrase in Chinese. 请把那个给我 means 'Please give me that one.' In casual speech, 那个 is often pronounced 'nèige' and even used as a filler word (like 'um' in English). The 把 construction here moves the object before the verb for emphasis.

items.question: _____件衣服多少钱？
items.translation: (How much is this piece of clothing?)
items.explanation: 这件 uses the measure word 件 (jiàn) for clothing items. 这件衣服多少钱？means 'How much is this piece of clothing?' When shopping in China, you'll constantly use 这 + measure word to point at items. Different items need different measure words: 这件 (clothing), 这双 (shoes/pairs), 这条 (pants/scarves).

items.question: _____边请坐
items.explanation: 这边 (zhèbiān) means 'this side/this way.' 这边请坐 means 'Please sit on this side' — a polite invitation. Direction words combine with demonstratives: 这边 (this side), 那边 (that side), 哪边 (which side). These are essential in restaurants, offices, and when giving directions. 这边请 ('this way please') is what hosts say to guide guests.

items.question: _____样做对不对？
items.translation: (Is it correct to do it this way?)
items.explanation: 这样 (zhèyàng) means 'this way/like this.' 这样做对不对？means 'Is it correct to do it this way?' The 样 (yàng, manner/way) creates manner demonstratives: 这样 (like this), 那样 (like that), 哪样 (which way). These are incredibly useful for asking about methods, comparing approaches, and giving instructions.

items.question: 我不喜欢_____种颜色
items.translation: (I don't like that kind of color)
items.explanation: 那种 (nàzhǒng) means 'that kind/type.' 我不喜欢那种颜色 means 'I don't like that kind of color.' The word 种 (zhǒng, kind/type) creates category demonstratives: 这种 (this kind), 那种 (that kind), 哪种 (which kind). Use these when discussing types, varieties, or categories of things rather than specific individual items.

items.question: _____会儿再说吧
items.explanation: 那会儿 can mean 'later' or 'at that time.' But here 那 functions more as 'then/in that case.' 那会儿再说吧 means 'Let's talk about it later then.' Chinese demonstratives extend beyond physical pointing — they reference time and abstract situations. 这会儿 means 'right now,' 那会儿 means 'at that time/later.'

items.question: 你要_____些东西？
items.explanation: 哪些 (nǎxiē) is the plural interrogative 'which ones/which things.' 你要哪些东西？means 'Which things do you want?' This is the plural counterpart of 哪个 (which one). Use 哪些 when asking someone to select multiple items from a group. The answer might use 这些 (these ones) or 那些 (those ones) to indicate the selection.

name: Greetings & Common Phrases
description: Essential everyday greetings, farewells, and polite expressions

items.question: 你_____！
items.explanation: 你好 (nǐ hǎo) is the most basic Chinese greeting, literally 'you good.' It's universally understood and appropriate in any situation. However, native speakers rarely use 你好 with close friends — it sounds somewhat formal. With friends, they might say 嗨 (hāi, hi) or 哎 (āi, hey). Use 你好 with strangers, in business, and as a safe default.

items.question: 谢谢你的_____
items.explanation: 帮助 (bāngzhù) means 'help.' 谢谢你的帮助 means 'Thank you for your help.' The structure 谢谢你的 + noun is a polite way to express gratitude for something specific. Other common completions: 谢谢你的关心 (thanks for your concern), 谢谢你的时间 (thanks for your time). Adding specificity makes your thanks feel more sincere and natural.

items.question: 请_____我
items.explanation: 原谅 (yuánliàng) means 'forgive/excuse.' 请原谅我 means 'Please forgive me.' For lighter apologies, 对不起 (duìbuqǐ, sorry) or 不好意思 (bùhǎoyìsi, excuse me/embarrassed) are more common. 不好意思 is especially useful — it covers everything from bumping into someone to arriving late. It's less heavy than 对不起.

items.question: 明天_____！
items.explanation: 明天见 (míngtiān jiàn) means 'See you tomorrow!' The word 见 (jiàn, to see/meet) is placed after the time word. This pattern works with any time: 下午见 (see you this afternoon), 周末见 (see you this weekend), 下次见 (see you next time). The most common farewell is simply 再见 (zàijiàn, goodbye — literally 'again see').

items.question: 不_____，这是我应该做的
items.translation: (You're welcome, this is what I should do)
items.explanation: 不客气 (bú kèqi) is the standard 'you're welcome,' literally 'don't be polite/formal.' 不客气，这是我应该做的 adds 'this is what I should do.' Other ways to say 'you're welcome': 没关系 (méi guānxi, no problem), 不用谢 (bú yòng xiè, no need to thank), or the casual 没事儿 (méishìr, it's nothing).

items.question: 你吃_____了吗？
items.explanation: 你吃饭了吗？literally asks 'Have you eaten?' but functions as a casual greeting — like 'How are you?' in English. This reflects Chinese culture where food is central to daily life and showing concern about someone's meals is a form of care. Don't be confused if someone asks this — they're being friendly, not necessarily inviting you to eat!

items.question: 请_____，王先生在吗？
items.explanation: 请问 (qǐngwèn) means 'may I ask' — an extremely polite way to begin a question or request. 请问，王先生在吗？means 'Excuse me, is Mr. Wang here?' Use 请问 before asking for directions, information, or when approaching strangers. It's much softer than jumping straight into a question and shows excellent manners.

items.question: 好久不_____了！
items.explanation: 好久不见 (hǎojiǔ bújiàn) literally means 'long time not see' — and yes, the English phrase 'long time no see' is believed to have originated from this Chinese expression! Use it when reuniting with someone after a long separation. It's warm, natural, and appropriate in both casual and semi-formal situations.

items.question: 慢_____走！
items.explanation: 慢慢走 (mànman zǒu) literally means 'slowly walk' but is used as a farewell meaning 'take care on your way.' Hosts say this to departing guests. The reduplication 慢慢 (slowly slowly) softens the tone. Similar farewell phrases: 路上小心 (lùshang xiǎoxīn, be careful on the road), 一路平安 (yílù píng'ān, safe journey).

items.question: 没_____，别担心
items.explanation: 没关系 (méi guānxi) means 'it doesn't matter / it's okay.' 没关系，别担心 adds 'don't worry.' This is one of the most important phrases to know — use it to reassure someone after they apologize, make a mistake, or express concern. Alternative: 没事 (méishì, it's nothing) is slightly more casual and equally common.

items.question: 认识你很_____
items.explanation: 认识你很高兴 (rènshi nǐ hěn gāoxìng) means 'Nice to meet you' — literally 'knowing you, very happy.' The verb 认识 (rènshi) means 'to know/recognize.' You can also say it the other way: 很高兴认识你 (very happy to know you). Both forms are equally natural. This phrase is essential at introductions, parties, and business meetings.

items.question: 你最近怎么_____？
items.explanation: 怎么样 (zěnmeyàng) means 'how is it / how about it.' 你最近怎么样？means 'How have you been recently?' This is a genuine inquiry about someone's wellbeing, not just a passing greeting. 最近 (zuìjìn, recently) specifies the time frame. Expected responses: 还好 (háihǎo, not bad), 挺好的 (tǐng hǎo de, pretty good), or 马马虎虎 (mǎmahūhū, so-so).

items.question: _____意思，我来晚了
items.explanation: 不好意思 (bùhǎoyìsi) expresses mild embarrassment or apology — it's lighter than 对不起 (duìbuqǐ, sorry). 不好意思，我来晚了 means 'Sorry, I'm late.' Use 不好意思 for minor inconveniences: being late, interrupting, asking for a favor. It literally means 'not good meaning/feeling' — expressing that you feel embarrassed about the situation.

items.question: 晚_____！好梦！
items.explanation: 晚安 (wǎn'ān) means 'good night' — literally 'evening peace.' It's used when parting ways at night or before bed. 好梦 (hǎo mèng) means 'good dreams/sweet dreams.' Time-of-day greetings in Chinese: 早上好 (zǎoshang hǎo, good morning), 下午好 (xiàwǔ hǎo, good afternoon), 晚上好 (wǎnshang hǎo, good evening), 晚安 (good night).

items.question: 祝你_____快乐！
items.explanation: 祝你生日快乐 (zhù nǐ shēngrì kuàilè) means 'Happy Birthday!' The verb 祝 (zhù) means 'to wish.' This pattern 祝你 + occasion + 快乐 works for many celebrations: 祝你新年快乐 (Happy New Year), 祝你节日快乐 (Happy Holidays). The song '祝你生日快乐' follows the same melody as 'Happy Birthday to You.'

name: Colors & Shapes
description: Describing colors, shapes, and visual characteristics in Chinese

items.question: 天空是_____的
items.explanation: 蓝色 (lánsè) means 'blue.' 天空是蓝色的 means 'The sky is blue.' Color words in Chinese are formed by adding 色 (sè, color) to the base color character: 蓝 + 色 = blue color. When used as adjectives, colors often end with 的: 蓝色的天空 (blue sky). The base character 蓝 alone can also function as the color in some contexts.

items.question: 这朵花是_____的
items.explanation: 红色 (hóngsè) means 'red' — the most culturally significant color in China. 红 represents luck, joy, and prosperity. Red envelopes (红包, hóngbāo) contain money gifts; brides wear red (红色婚纱); the Chinese flag is red. 这朵花是红色的 means 'This flower is red.' The measure word 朵 (duǒ) is specifically used for flowers and clouds.

items.question: 香蕉是_____的
items.explanation: 黄色 (huángsè) means 'yellow.' 香蕉是黄色的 means 'Bananas are yellow.' Historically, yellow (黄) was the emperor's exclusive color — commoners were forbidden from wearing it. Today, 黄 has an additional informal meaning: 黄色 can refer to 'adult/inappropriate content' (like 黄色电影, adult film). Context always makes the meaning clear.

items.question: 草地是_____的
items.explanation: 绿色 (lǜsè) means 'green.' 草地是绿色的 means 'The lawn is green.' The character 绿 has a tricky pronunciation — the 'ü' sound doesn't exist in English (like French 'u' or German 'ü'). Modern Chinese also uses 绿色 to mean 'eco-friendly/sustainable': 绿色食品 (green food/organic food), 绿色出行 (green travel/eco-friendly transportation).

items.question: 雪是_____的
items.explanation: 白色 (báisè) means 'white.' 雪是白色的 means 'Snow is white.' In Chinese culture, white has different associations than in Western culture — 白 is connected to mourning and funerals, not weddings. 白 also appears in many common words: 明白 (míngbai, understand), 白天 (báitiān, daytime), 白费 (báifèi, waste effort).

items.question: 头发是_____的
items.explanation: 黑色 (hēisè) means 'black.' 头发是黑色的 means 'Hair is black.' The character 黑 carries both literal and figurative meanings: 黑夜 (hēiyè, dark night), 黑暗 (hēi'àn, darkness), 黑市 (hēishì, black market). In Chinese internet slang, 黑 someone means to mock or trash-talk them — similar to English 'shade.'

items.question: 这个_____是圆的
items.explanation: 圆 (yuán) means 'round/circular.' 这个球是圆的 means 'This ball is round.' Shape adjectives in Chinese: 圆的 (round), 方的 (square), 长的 (long), 扁的 (flat). The character 圆 beautifully looks enclosed like a circle. 圆 also means 'complete/perfect' — 团圆 (tuányuán, reunion) and 圆满 (yuánmǎn, perfect/satisfactory).

items.question: 那个箱子是_____的
items.explanation: 方形 (fāngxíng) means 'square-shaped.' 那个箱子是方形的 means 'That box is square.' Shape names use 形 (xíng, shape/form): 圆形 (circular), 方形 (square), 三角形 (triangular), 长方形 (rectangular). The character 方 also means 'direction' and 'method' — it's one of the most versatile characters in Chinese.

items.question: 这件外套是_____的
items.explanation: 灰色 (huīsè) means 'gray.' 这件外套是灰色的 means 'This jacket is gray.' The character 灰 originally means 'ash/dust,' which makes sense — ash is gray! Chinese has specific compound colors too: 浅灰色 (qiǎn huīsè, light gray), 深灰色 (shēn huīsè, dark gray). Use 浅 (shallow/light) and 深 (deep/dark) to modify any color.

items.question: 她喜欢_____的裙子
items.explanation: 紫色 (zǐsè) means 'purple.' 她喜欢紫色的裙子 means 'She likes purple skirts.' In ancient China, purple was associated with nobility — the Forbidden City was also called 紫禁城 (Zǐjìnchéng, Purple Forbidden City). The adjective pattern is: color + 的 + noun. This 的 is essential when the color modifies a noun.

items.question: 这个桌子是_____的
items.explanation: 长方形 (chángfāngxíng) means 'rectangle' — literally 'long square shape.' 这个桌子是长方形的 means 'This table is rectangular.' Chinese shape words are often logical compounds: 长 (long) + 方 (square) + 形 (shape) = rectangle. Similarly, 正方形 (zhèngfāngxíng, 'proper square shape') means 'square' (equal sides).

items.question: 她穿了一件_____的衣服
items.translation: (She wore a pink piece of clothing)
items.explanation: 粉色 (fěnsè) means 'pink' — literally 'powder color.' 她穿了一件粉色的衣服 means 'She wore a pink outfit.' The character 粉 means 'powder/flour,' and pink is literally 'powder color' because face powder was traditionally pinkish. Other compound colors: 橙色 (chéngsè, orange), 棕色 (zōngsè, brown), 金色 (jīnsè, gold).

items.question: 交通标志是_____的
items.explanation: 三角形 (sānjiǎoxíng) means 'triangle' — literally 'three angle shape.' 交通标志是三角形的 means 'Traffic signs are triangular.' The logic is transparent: 三 (three) + 角 (angle/corner) + 形 (shape). This systematic naming makes Chinese shapes easy to learn. Pentagon would be 五角形 (five-angle shape), hexagon 六角形 (six-angle shape).

items.question: 深_____比浅蓝好看
items.translation: (Dark blue looks better than light blue)
items.explanation: 深蓝 (shēn lán) means 'dark blue' and 浅蓝 (qiǎn lán) means 'light blue.' The modifiers 深 (deep/dark) and 浅 (shallow/light) can precede any color. 深蓝比浅蓝好看 means 'Dark blue looks better than light blue.' This comparison uses 比 (bǐ) — the standard comparison structure: A + 比 + B + adjective.

items.question: 彩虹有七种_____
items.explanation: 颜色 (yánsè) means 'color.' 彩虹有七种颜色 means 'A rainbow has seven colors.' 彩虹 (cǎihóng, rainbow) literally means 'colorful arch.' The measure word 种 (zhǒng, kind/type) is used with colors because each color is a 'type.' You'll see 颜色 in practical questions like 你喜欢什么颜色？(What color do you like?) — essential for shopping!

name: Family Terms
description: Chinese family relationship terms — one of the most detailed systems in any language

items.question: 她是我的_____
items.explanation: 妈妈 (māma) means 'mother/mom.' Chinese has one of the world's most complex kinship systems, but 妈妈 is universal and simple. The reduplicated form (妈妈 instead of just 妈) is the affectionate, everyday term. More formal alternatives: 母亲 (mǔqīn, mother — formal/written) and 妈 (mā, mom — casual). In Chinese culture, mothers are deeply respected.

items.question: 他是我的_____
items.explanation: 爸爸 (bàba) means 'father/dad.' Like 妈妈, the reduplicated form is casual and warm. Formal term: 父亲 (fùqīn, father). In Chinese families, the father traditionally holds authority, reflected in the phrase 严父慈母 (yán fù cí mǔ, strict father, kind mother). Modern Chinese families are evolving, but respect for parents remains central.

items.question: 我_____比我大三岁
items.translation: (My older brother is three years older than me)
items.explanation: 哥哥 (gēge) means 'older brother.' Chinese distinguishes between older and younger siblings — there's no single word for just 'brother.' 哥哥 (older brother) vs. 弟弟 (dìdi, younger brother). 我哥哥比我大三岁 means 'My older brother is three years older than me.' This distinction reflects the importance of age hierarchy in Chinese culture.

items.question: 我_____今年五岁了
items.translation: (My younger sister is five years old this year)
items.explanation: 妹妹 (mèimei) means 'younger sister.' 我妹妹今年五岁了 means 'My younger sister is five years old this year.' The four sibling terms form a clear system: 哥哥 (older brother), 姐姐 (jiějie, older sister), 弟弟 (dìdi, younger brother), 妹妹 (younger sister). Each carries different social expectations in traditional families.

items.question: _____做的菜最好吃
items.explanation: 奶奶 (nǎinai) means 'paternal grandmother' — father's mother. Chinese has separate terms for maternal and paternal grandparents! 奶奶 (father's mother) vs. 姥姥/外婆 (lǎolao/wàipó, mother's mother). 奶奶做的菜最好吃 means 'Grandma's cooking is the best.' This distinction exists because the paternal family historically held primary importance.

items.question: 我_____是老师
items.explanation: 爷爷 (yéye) means 'paternal grandfather' — father's father. 我爷爷是老师 means 'My grandfather is a teacher.' The paternal grandparents (爷爷/奶奶) are distinguished from maternal grandparents (姥爷/外公 and 姥姥/外婆). In northern China, 姥爷/姥姥 are common; in southern China, 外公/外婆 are preferred.

items.question: 我_____在北京工作
items.translation: (My uncle (father's older brother) works in Beijing)
items.explanation: 伯伯 (bóbo) means 'father's older brother.' Chinese has distinct terms for EVERY uncle: 伯伯 (father's older brother), 叔叔 (shūshu, father's younger brother), 舅舅 (jiùjiu, mother's brother), 姑父 (gūfu, father's sister's husband), 姨夫 (yífu, mother's sister's husband). This system precisely maps every family relationship.

items.question: _____给我买了新玩具
items.translation: (Uncle (father's younger brother) bought me a new toy)
items.explanation: 叔叔 (shūshu) means 'father's younger brother.' 叔叔给我买了新玩具 means 'Uncle bought me a new toy.' Interestingly, 叔叔 is also used as a polite term children use for any adult male — similar to 'uncle' in some Asian cultures. A child might call a stranger 叔叔 as a sign of respect and friendliness.

items.question: 我_____有两个孩子
items.explanation: 阿姨 (āyí) means 'mother's sister' but has expanded to a general term for women of your mother's generation. 我阿姨有两个孩子 means 'My aunt has two children.' Like 叔叔, 阿姨 is widely used as a polite address — children call female strangers 阿姨, and it's also the term for housekeepers/nannies.

items.question: 你的_____做什么工作？
items.translation: (What does your husband/wife do for work?)
items.explanation: 丈夫 (zhàngfu) means 'husband.' The formal pair is 丈夫/妻子 (zhàngfu/qīzi, husband/wife). Casual terms: 老公 (lǎogōng, hubby) and 老婆 (lǎopo, wifey). The character 丈 originally meant 'ten Chinese feet' — a tall, strong man. Modern couples increasingly use 先生 (xiānsheng, Mr./husband) and 太太 (tàitai, Mrs./wife) in formal introductions.

items.question: 她是我_____的女儿
items.explanation: 姐姐 (jiějie) means 'older sister.' 她是我姐姐的女儿 means 'She is my older sister's daughter.' In Chinese, that niece would be called 外甥女 (wàishēngnǚ, sister's daughter) or 侄女 (zhínǚ, brother's daughter). Yes, Chinese even distinguishes nieces and nephews based on which sibling they come from!

items.question: 我_____下个月结婚
items.translation: (My younger brother is getting married next month)
items.explanation: 弟弟 (dìdi) means 'younger brother.' 我弟弟下个月结婚 means 'My younger brother is getting married next month.' The verb 结婚 (jiéhūn, get married) literally means 'tie/knot marriage.' In traditional Chinese culture, the older siblings were expected to marry first — 弟弟 marrying before 哥哥 might raise eyebrows in conservative families.

items.question: 我有一个_____和一个女儿
items.translation: (I have a son and a daughter)
items.explanation: 儿子 (érzi) means 'son.' 我有一个儿子和一个女儿 means 'I have a son and a daughter.' The character 儿 (ér) means 'child/son' and appears in many compound words. 女儿 (nǚ'ér) means 'daughter' — literally 'female child.' The general term for children regardless of gender is 孩子 (háizi).

items.question: _____是妈妈的妈妈
items.explanation: 姥姥 (lǎolao) means 'maternal grandmother' — mother's mother, used primarily in northern China. Southern speakers use 外婆 (wàipó) instead. 姥姥是妈妈的妈妈 clearly illustrates the paternal/maternal distinction. The prefix 外 (wài, outside) in 外婆/外公 historically reflected the idea that the mother's family was 'outside' the patrilineal family.

items.question: 全_____人一起吃年夜饭
items.translation: (The whole family eats New Year's Eve dinner together)
items.explanation: 全家 (quánjiā) means 'the whole family.' 全家人一起吃年夜饭 means 'The whole family eats New Year's Eve dinner together.' 年夜饭 (niányèfàn, New Year's Eve dinner) is the most important family meal of the year in Chinese culture. Family reunion (团圆, tuányuán) is the core value of Spring Festival — millions travel home for this meal.

name: Daily Routines
description: Describing everyday activities, schedules, and habits in Chinese

items.question: 我每天早上七点_____
items.explanation: 起床 (qǐchuáng) means 'to get up/get out of bed.' 我每天早上七点起床 means 'I get up at 7 every morning.' The time structure follows Chinese word order: 每天 (every day) + 早上 (morning) + 七点 (7 o'clock) + verb. Time words always come BEFORE the verb in Chinese, which is opposite to English placement.

items.question: 起床以后我先_____
items.translation: (After getting up, I first brush my teeth)
items.explanation: 刷牙 (shuāyá) means 'to brush teeth.' 起床以后我先刷牙 means 'After getting up, I first brush my teeth.' The connector 以后 (yǐhòu, after) links two actions in sequence. 先 (xiān, first) indicates priority. Morning routine vocabulary: 刷牙 (brush teeth), 洗脸 (xǐliǎn, wash face), 梳头 (shūtóu, comb hair), 穿衣服 (chuān yīfu, get dressed).

items.question: 我八点_____上班
items.explanation: 去上班 (qù shàngbān) means 'to go to work.' 我八点去上班 means 'I go to work at 8.' The verb 去 (qù, to go) indicates movement away from the speaker. Compare: 去上班 (go to work), 去上学 (qù shàngxué, go to school), 去超市 (qù chāoshì, go to the supermarket). The time word 八点 sits before the verb phrase.

items.question: 中午我们一起_____午饭
items.explanation: 吃午饭 (chī wǔfàn) means 'to eat lunch.' Chinese meal names: 早饭/早餐 (zǎofàn/zǎocān, breakfast), 午饭/午餐 (wǔfàn/wǔcān, lunch), 晚饭/晚餐 (wǎnfàn/wǎncān, dinner). The 饭 versions are casual, 餐 versions are formal. 一起 (yìqǐ, together) adds a social dimension — eating together is central to Chinese culture.

items.question: 下午三点我_____咖啡
items.explanation: 喝 (hē) means 'to drink.' 下午三点我喝咖啡 means 'At 3 PM I drink coffee.' Chinese distinguishes 吃 (chī, eat — for food) from 喝 (hē, drink — for liquids). However, there's an exception: 吃药 (chīyào, take medicine) uses 吃 even though you swallow it. 喝 is used for: 喝水 (drink water), 喝茶 (drink tea), 喝酒 (drink alcohol).

items.question: 晚上我喜欢_____电视
items.translation: (In the evening I like to watch TV)
items.explanation: 看电视 (kàn diànshì) means 'to watch TV.' 晚上我喜欢看电视 means 'In the evening I like to watch TV.' The verb 看 (kàn) means 'to look/see/watch/read.' It's incredibly versatile: 看书 (kàn shū, read a book), 看电影 (kàn diànyǐng, watch a movie), 看医生 (kàn yīshēng, see a doctor), 看朋友 (kàn péngyou, visit friends).

items.question: 我通常十一点_____
items.explanation: 睡觉 (shuìjiào) means 'to go to sleep/go to bed.' 我通常十一点睡觉 means 'I usually go to bed at 11.' The adverb 通常 (tōngcháng, usually) indicates habitual actions. Frequency adverbs in daily routines: 总是 (zǒngshì, always), 经常 (jīngcháng, often), 有时候 (yǒu shíhou, sometimes), 很少 (hěn shǎo, rarely), 从不 (cóng bù, never).

items.question: 星期六我_____打扫房间
items.explanation: 会 (huì) here indicates habitual future action — 'I will (habitually).' 星期六我会打扫房间 means 'On Saturdays I clean my room.' 打扫 (dǎsǎo) means 'to clean/sweep.' Weekend routines: 打扫房间 (clean room), 洗衣服 (xǐ yīfu, do laundry), 买菜 (mǎi cài, buy groceries), 做饭 (zuòfàn, cook). The 会 adds a sense of regularity.

items.question: 早饭我一般吃_____
items.explanation: 面包 (miànbāo) means 'bread.' 早饭我一般吃面包 means 'For breakfast I usually eat bread.' Traditional Chinese breakfast is quite different from Western: 油条 (yóutiáo, fried dough sticks), 豆浆 (dòujiāng, soy milk), 包子 (bāozi, steamed buns), 粥 (zhōu, congee). The adverb 一般 (yìbān, generally/usually) is slightly less strong than 通常.

items.question: 我每天_____半个小时的步
items.translation: (I walk for half an hour every day)
items.explanation: 散步 (sànbù) means 'to take a walk/stroll.' 我每天散半个小时的步 means 'I walk for half an hour every day.' Notice the verb-object structure gets split: 散...步 with the duration 半个小时 (half an hour) inserted in between. This splitting pattern (离合词, líhécí) is a unique Chinese grammar feature that trips up many learners.

items.question: 下班以后我去_____
items.translation: (After work I go to the gym)
items.explanation: 健身房 (jiànshēnfáng) means 'gym' — literally 'fitness room.' 下班以后我去健身房 means 'After work I go to the gym.' 下班 (xiàbān, get off work) pairs with 上班 (shàngbān, go to work). The 以后 connector creates a clear after-before sequence. Exercise vocabulary: 跑步 (pǎobù, run), 游泳 (yóuyǒng, swim), 瑜伽 (yújiā, yoga).

items.question: 我每天_____八个小时
items.explanation: 睡 (shuì) means 'to sleep.' 我每天睡八个小时 means 'I sleep eight hours every day.' Notice here we use 睡 alone (not 睡觉) because of the duration complement. When a duration follows, the verb-object compound often drops the object. The duration phrase 八个小时 (eight hours) uses 个 as a measure word for hours — colloquial and common.

items.question: 周末我喜欢_____朋友
items.translation: (On weekends I like to meet friends)
items.explanation: 见朋友 (jiàn péngyou) means 'to meet/see friends.' 周末我喜欢见朋友 means 'On weekends I like to meet friends.' 见 (jiàn) implies a planned meeting, while 看 (kàn) is more like visiting. Weekend activities: 见朋友 (meet friends), 逛街 (guàngjiē, go shopping/window shopping), 看电影 (watch movies), 爬山 (páshān, hike).

items.question: 我每天_____两次澡
items.explanation: 洗澡 (xǐzǎo) means 'to bathe/shower.' 我每天洗两次澡 means 'I shower twice every day.' Like 散步, this verb-object compound splits when you insert frequency: 洗...澡 with 两次 (twice) in between. Hygiene vocabulary: 洗澡 (bathe), 洗头 (xǐtóu, wash hair), 洗手 (xǐshǒu, wash hands). The verb 洗 (wash) is extremely productive.

items.question: 我每天坐_____去上班
items.translation: (I take the subway to work every day)
items.explanation: 地铁 (dìtiě) means 'subway/metro.' 我每天坐地铁去上班 means 'I take the subway to work every day.' The verb 坐 (zuò, to sit) is how you 'take' transportation in Chinese: 坐地铁 (take subway), 坐公交车 (take bus), 坐出租车 (take taxi), 坐飞机 (take airplane). Exception: 开车 (kāichē, drive a car) uses 开 (drive/operate).

name: 把 (Bǎ) Construction
description: Using the 把 structure to express disposal or directed action on an object

items.question: 请你_____书放在桌子上。
items.translation: (Please put the book on the table.)
items.explanation: 把 (bǎ) is used to indicate what happens to a specific object. 请你把书放在桌子上 means 'Please put the book on the table.' The 把 construction follows the pattern: Subject + 把 + Object + Verb + Complement. It brings the object BEFORE the verb to show that an action is being done TO that specific object, resulting in some change or displacement.

items.question: 他把门_____了。
items.explanation: 关 (guān) means 'to close.' 他把门关了 means 'He closed the door.' In the 把 construction, the verb must have a result or completion — you cannot say 他把门关 alone without 了 or another complement. The 了 here indicates the action was completed. This is a classic 把 sentence because the door undergoes a change of state (from open to closed).

items.question: 她把衣服_____干净了。
items.explanation: 洗 (xǐ) means 'to wash.' 她把衣服洗干净了 means 'She washed the clothes clean.' Here 干净 (gānjìng, clean) is a result complement showing the outcome of the washing. The 把 construction is required here because we're describing what happened to the clothes — they were washed and became clean. The verb + result complement combination is very common in 把 sentences.

items.question: 我把作业_____完了。
items.explanation: 做 (zuò) means 'to do.' 我把作业做完了 means 'I finished doing the homework.' The complement 完 (wán, finished) shows the action reached completion. The 把 construction emphasizes the disposal of the homework — it has been dealt with and is now done. This is one of the most common everyday uses of 把, expressing that a task has been completed.

items.question: 请把窗户_____开。
items.explanation: 打开 (dǎkāi) means 'to open.' 请把窗户打开 means 'Please open the window.' Here 打 is the verb and 开 is the result complement meaning 'open.' The 把 construction is natural with commands because you're directing someone to do something specific to an object. Note that 打 alone means 'to hit/strike,' but 打开 as a compound means 'to open.'

items.question: 弟弟把杯子_____碎了。
items.translation: (Little brother broke the cup into pieces.)
items.explanation: 打碎 (dǎ suì) means 'to smash/break into pieces.' 弟弟把杯子打碎了 means 'Little brother broke the cup into pieces.' The 把 construction is ideal here because the cup underwent a dramatic change — from whole to broken. 碎 (suì, shattered) is the result complement describing the outcome. This pattern is very common when describing accidents or unintended consequences.

items.question: 妈妈把饭_____好了。
items.explanation: 做好 (zuòhǎo) means 'to finish making/preparing.' 妈妈把饭做好了 means 'Mom has finished cooking the meal.' The complement 好 (hǎo) here doesn't mean 'good' but indicates completion — the action has been done properly and is ready. This is a very natural daily-life use of the 把 construction, emphasizing that the meal has been handled and is now ready to eat.

items.question: 他把钱_____给了朋友。
items.translation: (He gave the money to his friend.)
items.explanation: 送 (sòng) means 'to give (as a gift)/to send.' 他把钱送给了朋友 means 'He gave the money to his friend.' The 把 construction here shows the transfer of the money from the subject to the friend. 给 serves as a directional complement indicating the recipient. This pattern — 把 + Object + Verb + 给 + Person — is extremely common for describing transfers of ownership.

items.question: 老师让我们把课文_____三遍。
items.translation: (The teacher told us to read the text three times.)
items.explanation: 读 (dú) means 'to read aloud.' 老师让我们把课文读三遍 means 'The teacher told us to read the text three times.' Here 三遍 (sān biàn, three times) is a frequency complement. The 把 construction is used because there's a specific action done to the text (reading it a set number of times). 遍 is a measure word specifically for counting how many times an action is performed from start to finish.

items.question: 你能把这个句子_____成英文吗？
items.translation: (Can you translate this sentence into English?)
items.explanation: 翻译 (fānyì) means 'to translate.' 你能把这个句子翻译成英文吗 means 'Can you translate this sentence into English?' The complement 成 (chéng) means 'into/become' and shows the result of the translation. The 把 construction works perfectly here because the sentence is being transformed from one language into another — a clear case of something being acted upon and changed.

items.question: 他把自行车_____在学校了。
items.explanation: 忘 (wàng) means 'to forget/leave behind.' 他把自行车忘在学校了 means 'He left (forgot) the bicycle at school.' The 把 construction with 在 + location shows where the object ended up. This is a very practical pattern: 把 + Object + Verb + 在 + Place, which is used whenever you want to express that something was placed, left, or ended up somewhere specific.

items.question: 请把你的名字_____在这里。
items.explanation: 写 (xiě) means 'to write.' 请把你的名字写在这里 means 'Please write your name here.' This follows the 把 + Object + Verb + 在 + Place pattern. The 把 construction is natural here because the name is being placed (written) onto a specific location. In forms, official documents, and everyday instructions, this pattern appears constantly.

items.question: 她把头发_____短了。
items.explanation: 剪 (jiǎn) means 'to cut (with scissors).' 她把头发剪短了 means 'She cut her hair short.' 短 (duǎn, short) is a result complement describing the state after cutting. The 把 construction is essential here because the hair underwent a physical change — from long to short. This pattern is common when describing changes in appearance or physical modifications.

items.question: 我想把这个消息_____诉他。
items.translation: (I want to tell him this news.)
items.explanation: 告诉 (gàosu) means 'to tell/inform.' 我想把这个消息告诉他 means 'I want to tell him this news.' The blank takes 告 because 告诉 is split across the blank and the character after it (诉). The 把 construction is used because the news is being transferred to the person — information is being 'disposed of' by passing it along. This is a very common pattern for communication verbs.

items.question: 别把这件事_____了。
items.explanation: 忘 (wàng) means 'to forget.' 别把这件事忘了 means 'Don't forget this matter.' 别 (bié) means 'don't' — this is a negative command. The 把 construction works with negation by placing 别/不要 before 把. This pattern is very common in daily reminders and warnings. Note: you can also say 别忘了这件事, but the 把 version places more emphasis on the specific matter being discussed.

name: 被 (Bèi) Passive Voice
description: Expressing passive actions using the 被 construction in Chinese

items.question: 我的手机_____偷了。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive voice in Chinese. 我的手机被偷了 means 'My phone was stolen.' Unlike English where passive voice is very common, Chinese uses 被 mainly for unfortunate or undesirable events. The basic pattern is: Object/Receiver + 被 + (Agent) + Verb. Here the agent (thief) is omitted because it's unknown. This is one of the most important intermediate grammar structures.

items.question: 那本书_____他借走了。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks passive voice. 那本书被他借走了 means 'That book was borrowed (and taken away) by him.' The full pattern here is: Subject + 被 + Agent + Verb + Complement. 借走 (jiè zǒu) combines 'borrow' with the directional complement 走 (away). In 被 sentences, the verb almost always needs a complement (了, 走, 完, etc.) — a bare verb after 被 is usually ungrammatical.

items.question: 蛋糕_____妹妹吃了。
items.translation: (The cake was eaten by little sister.)
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) introduces the agent in a passive sentence. 蛋糕被妹妹吃了 means 'The cake was eaten by little sister.' This follows the pattern: Subject + 被 + Agent + Verb + 了. The 被 construction often implies the speaker is unhappy about the situation — perhaps they wanted the cake themselves! This emotional undertone of 被 is an important distinction from English passive voice.

items.question: 他_____老师批评了。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 他被老师批评了 means 'He was criticized by the teacher.' 批评 (pīpíng) means 'to criticize.' This is a classic 被 sentence because being criticized is an unpleasant experience. Traditional Chinese grammar reserves 被 for negative situations, though modern Chinese increasingly uses it for neutral events too (e.g., 被选为 'was elected as').

items.question: 花瓶_____打碎了。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 花瓶被打碎了 means 'The vase was broken.' Here the agent is omitted (we don't know or don't want to say who broke it). 打碎 (dǎ suì) means 'to break into pieces.' When the agent is unknown or unimportant, you simply use: Subject + 被 + Verb + Complement. This agentless passive is very common in everyday Chinese.

items.question: 这个问题已经_____解决了。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) creates the passive. 这个问题已经被解决了 means 'This problem has already been solved.' 已经 (yǐjīng, already) comes before 被. 解决 (jiějué) means 'to solve/resolve.' This is an example of 被 used in a neutral/positive context, which is increasingly common in modern Chinese, especially in news, academic, and formal writing.

items.question: 小偷_____警察抓住了。
items.translation: (The thief was caught by the police.)
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 小偷被警察抓住了 means 'The thief was caught by the police.' 抓住 (zhuā zhù) means 'to catch/grab and hold.' The complement 住 indicates the action was successful and sustained. This is another example of neutral/positive 被 usage — catching a thief is a good thing. The pattern: Subject + 被 + Agent + Verb + Result Complement is very productive.

items.question: 我的车_____撞坏了。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 我的车被撞坏了 means 'My car was hit and damaged.' 撞坏 (zhuàng huài) combines 'hit/collide' with 'broken/damaged.' This is a quintessential 被 sentence — something bad happened to the speaker's car. The negative emotional connotation is strong here. Compare with the 把 version: 有人把我的车撞坏了 (Someone damaged my car), which focuses on the doer's action.

items.question: 这首歌_____很多人喜欢。
items.translation: (This song is liked by many people.)
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 这首歌被很多人喜欢 means 'This song is liked by many people.' While traditional grammar says 被 is for negative events, modern Chinese freely uses it for positive situations too. 喜欢 (xǐhuan, to like) with 被 shows a newer usage pattern. However, many native speakers might prefer the active voice here: 很多人喜欢这首歌 (Many people like this song).

items.question: 信_____寄出去了。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 信被寄出去了 means 'The letter has been sent out.' 寄出去 (jì chūqù) combines 'mail' with the directional complement 出去 (out). The agent is omitted because who sent it isn't important — what matters is that it's been sent. This is a common pattern in business and daily communication contexts.

items.question: 他的建议_____采纳了。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 他的建议被采纳了 means 'His suggestion was adopted.' 采纳 (cǎinà) means 'to adopt/accept (a suggestion).' This is a formal, neutral use of 被 common in workplace and academic settings. The agent is omitted because the focus is on the suggestion being accepted, not on who accepted it. This pattern appears frequently in meeting summaries and reports.

items.question: 那个学生_____选为班长了。
items.translation: (That student was elected as class monitor.)
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 那个学生被选为班长了 means 'That student was elected as class monitor.' 选为 (xuǎn wéi) means 'to be elected/chosen as.' This is a clearly positive use of 被, showing how modern Chinese has expanded the passive beyond negative events. The 为 (wéi, as) connects the verb to the resulting title or role.

items.question: 孩子_____妈妈叫醒了。
items.translation: (The child was woken up by mom.)
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 孩子被妈妈叫醒了 means 'The child was woken up by mom.' 叫醒 (jiào xǐng) means 'to call awake/wake up.' The result complement 醒 (xǐng, awake) shows the outcome. This could be seen as slightly negative (the child didn't want to be woken up). Compare: 妈妈把孩子叫醒了 uses 把 and focuses on what mom did, while the 被 version focuses on what happened to the child.

items.question: 作业没有_____做完。
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 作业没有被做完 means 'The homework hasn't been finished.' With negation, 没有 comes before 被. The pattern is: Subject + 没有 + 被 + Verb + Complement. This is important because many learners incorrectly place 没有 after 被. Note also that 不 is rarely used with 被 — since 被 sentences describe events that happen/happened, 没有 (didn't/hasn't) is the natural negator.

items.question: 窗户_____风吹开了。
items.translation: (The window was blown open by the wind.)
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) marks the passive. 窗户被风吹开了 means 'The window was blown open by the wind.' 风 (fēng, wind) is the agent, 吹 (chuī, blow) is the verb, and 开 (kāi, open) is the result complement. The 被 construction can have natural forces as agents, not just people. This sentence demonstrates how 被 handles non-human agents naturally, unlike some other passive constructions.

name: Result Complements
description: Using verb complements to express the result or outcome of an action

items.question: 我听_____了，请再说一遍。
items.translation: (I didn't hear clearly, please say it again.)
items.explanation: 听不清楚 (tīng bù qīngchǔ) means 'cannot hear clearly.' Result complements attach directly after the verb to show the outcome. 清楚 (qīngchǔ, clear) describes the result of listening. The negative form uses 不 between verb and complement: 听不清楚. This is different from 没听清楚 (didn't hear clearly — past event) vs. 听不清楚 (can't hear clearly — ability). This distinction is crucial for intermediate learners.

items.question: 这本书我看_____了。
items.explanation: 看完 (kàn wán) means 'to finish reading.' 完 (wán) is one of the most common result complements, meaning 'finished/completed.' It can attach to almost any action verb: 吃完 (finish eating), 做完 (finish doing), 写完 (finish writing). The structure is simply: Verb + 完 + 了. This complement is essential for expressing that an action has been carried through to completion.

items.question: 你找_____他了吗？
items.explanation: 找到 (zhǎo dào) means 'to find (successfully).' 到 (dào) as a result complement means 'reached/achieved the goal.' Compare: 找 means 'to look for' (the process), while 找到 means 'to find' (the successful result). This distinction is vital — 我找了他 means 'I looked for him,' while 我找到了他 means 'I found him.' Other examples: 买到 (managed to buy), 学到 (learned successfully).

items.question: 我没看_____那个标志。
items.explanation: 看见 (kàn jiàn) means 'to see (perceive visually).' 见 (jiàn) as a result complement means 'perceived/noticed.' Compare: 看 means 'to look,' while 看见 means 'to see (successfully notice).' Similarly: 听见 (tīng jiàn) means 'to hear.' The negative past form uses 没: 没看见 (didn't see). This complement is about whether perception was achieved, not about ability.

items.question: 饭做_____了，可以吃了。
items.translation: (The meal is ready, we can eat now.)
items.explanation: 做好 (zuò hǎo) means 'finished making/prepared properly.' 好 (hǎo) as a result complement means 'completed properly/ready.' It differs from 完 (simply finished) — 好 implies the result is satisfactory. 做好了 means 'done and ready,' while 做完了 just means 'finished doing.' Other examples: 准备好 (prepared and ready), 安排好 (arranged properly). This subtle difference is important for natural Chinese.

items.question: 这个字我写_____了。
items.explanation: 写错 (xiě cuò) means 'to write incorrectly.' 错 (cuò, wrong) is a result complement showing the action went wrong. This is an extremely common and useful complement: 说错 (said wrong), 做错 (did wrong), 买错 (bought the wrong thing), 走错 (went the wrong way). The positive counterpart is 对 (duì): 做对了 (did it right). These complements express judgment about the outcome of an action.

items.question: 孩子们跑_____了。
items.explanation: 跑累 (pǎo lèi) means 'tired from running.' 累 (lèi, tired) is a result complement describing the physical state after the action. This pattern is very natural in Chinese: the verb shows what caused the condition, and the complement shows the resulting state. More examples: 站累了 (tired from standing), 走累了 (tired from walking), 学累了 (tired from studying). The 了 indicates the state has been reached.

items.question: 水果洗_____了，可以吃了。
items.translation: (The fruit has been washed clean, ready to eat.)
items.explanation: 洗干净 (xǐ gānjìng) means 'washed clean.' 干净 (gānjìng, clean) is a result complement describing the state after washing. Two-syllable result complements like 干净, 清楚, 明白 are common and very descriptive. They paint a complete picture: the action (wash) + the result (clean). Compare: 洗完 (finished washing — might not be clean) vs. 洗干净 (washed and is now clean).

items.question: 老师讲的我都听_____了。
items.explanation: 听懂 (tīng dǒng) means 'to understand by listening.' 懂 (dǒng, understand) is a result complement showing comprehension was achieved. This is one of the most useful complements for language learners: 听懂 (understood what was heard), 看懂 (understood what was read), 学懂 (understood what was studied). The negative form 听不懂 (can't understand) is a phrase every Chinese learner uses daily!

items.question: 雨把衣服淋_____了。
items.explanation: 淋湿 (lín shī) means 'to get wet from rain.' 湿 (shī, wet) is a result complement describing the state after being rained on. 淋 (lín) means 'to drench/pour upon.' This sentence also uses the 把 construction (rain 把 clothes drench-wet), showing how result complements often combine with 把. The 了 confirms the change of state has occurred. Other state complements: 弄脏 (made dirty), 弄破 (tore/damaged).

items.question: 这道题我算_____了。
items.explanation: 算对 (suàn duì) means 'calculated correctly.' 对 (duì, correct/right) is the positive counterpart of 错. Together they form a pair of result complements for expressing accuracy: 做对 (did right) vs. 做错 (did wrong), 猜对 (guessed right) vs. 猜错 (guessed wrong), 答对 (answered correctly) vs. 答错 (answered incorrectly). This complement is especially common in school and exam contexts.

items.question: 门锁_____了，进不去。
items.translation: (The door is locked, can't get in.)
items.explanation: 锁住 (suǒ zhù) means 'locked securely.' 住 (zhù) as a result complement means 'firmly/securely/held in place.' It implies something is fixed or immovable: 记住 (memorize firmly), 抓住 (grab and hold), 站住 (stand still/stop). The 进不去 (can't get in) is a directional potential complement showing the consequence. This complement often appears in pairs with 不 to show inability due to being stuck.

items.question: 你说的我记_____了。
items.explanation: 记不住 (jì bù zhù) means 'cannot remember/retain.' This is the negative potential form of the result complement 住: Verb + 不 + 住 means 'cannot hold onto/retain.' Compare: 记住 (remembered firmly) vs. 记不住 (can't remember). The potential form inserts 不 (inability) or 得 (ability) between verb and complement. Other examples: 拿不住 (can't hold onto), 站不住 (can't stand steady).

items.question: 我吃_____了，不想再吃了。
items.translation: (I'm full, I don't want to eat anymore.)
items.explanation: 吃饱 (chī bǎo) means 'to eat until full.' 饱 (bǎo, full/satiated) is a result complement describing the state after eating. This is an extremely common daily expression. 你吃饱了吗？(Have you eaten your fill?) is a very natural way to ask if someone has had enough to eat. Compare: 吃完 (finished eating — plate is empty) vs. 吃饱 (ate enough — stomach is full). The distinction matters!

items.question: 太小了，我看_____。
items.translation: (It's too small, I can't see it clearly.)
items.explanation: 看不清 (kàn bù qīng) means 'cannot see clearly.' 清 (qīng) is short for 清楚 (clear) and serves as a result complement. The negative potential form 看不清 expresses inability to achieve clear vision. This shortened form is very colloquial — you'll hear 看不清 more often than 看不清楚 in daily speech. Similar shortened forms: 听不清 (can't hear clearly), 说不清 (can't explain clearly). These are must-know expressions.

name: Direction Complements
description: Using directional verb complements to indicate movement direction after actions

items.question: 他走_____教室了。
items.explanation: 走进 (zǒu jìn) means 'to walk into.' 进 (jìn, enter/into) is a simple direction complement showing movement from outside to inside. The basic directional complements are: 进 (in), 出 (out), 上 (up), 下 (down), 回 (back), 过 (across), 起 (up/rise). They attach directly after the verb to show the direction of the action. This is one of the most frequently used grammar patterns in Chinese.

items.question: 请你拿_____来给我看看。
items.translation: (Please bring it over for me to have a look.)
items.explanation: 拿过来 (ná guòlái) means 'to bring over (toward the speaker).' 过来 (guòlái) is a compound direction complement meaning 'come over/toward the speaker.' The compound directional complements combine a simple direction (过, 进, 出, etc.) with 来 (toward speaker) or 去 (away from speaker). 拿过来 = bring here, 拿过去 = take there. This 来/去 distinction is essential for natural Chinese.

items.question: 他跑_____楼了。
items.explanation: 跑上楼 (pǎo shàng lóu) means 'to run upstairs.' 上 (shàng, up/ascend) is a direction complement showing upward movement. When an object (楼, building/floor) is present, it goes between the simple direction complement and 来/去: 跑上楼来 (ran upstairs toward speaker) or 跑上楼去 (ran upstairs away from speaker). The location object splits the compound complement.

items.question: 孩子从树上跳_____了。
items.translation: (The child jumped down from the tree.)
