items.explanation: 跳下来 (tiào xiàlái) means 'to jump down (toward the speaker).' 下来 (xiàlái) is a compound direction complement meaning 'downward toward the speaker.' The 来 tells us the child is jumping toward the speaker's position. If the speaker were below and the child jumped down away from them, you'd use 跳下去. This perspective-based choice between 来 and 去 is a distinctive feature of Chinese directional complements.

items.question: 他站_____了迎接客人。
items.translation: (He stood up to greet the guests.)
items.explanation: 站起来 (zhàn qǐlái) means 'to stand up.' 起来 (qǐlái) as a direction complement means 'upward from a lower position.' It's used with verbs to show rising or beginning: 站起来 (stand up), 坐起来 (sit up), 拿起来 (pick up). Beyond physical direction, 起来 has extended meanings: 想起来 (recall/remember), 笑起来 (burst out laughing) — showing the beginning of an action or mental process.

items.question: 快把伞收_____吧。
items.explanation: 收起来 (shōu qǐlái) means 'to put away/store.' This is an extended use of 起来 meaning 'away into storage.' Other examples: 藏起来 (hide away), 包起来 (wrap up), 锁起来 (lock up). The 起来 here doesn't mean 'upward' literally — it means 'gathering together and storing.' This figurative extension of directional complements is very common and adds richness to the language.

items.question: 老师走_____教室了。
items.translation: (The teacher walked out of the classroom.)
items.explanation: 走出 (zǒu chū) means 'to walk out of.' 出 (chū, exit/out) is a direction complement showing movement from inside to outside. Compare: 走进教室 (walk into the classroom) vs. 走出教室 (walk out of the classroom). The direction complement completely changes the meaning while the main verb (走, walk) stays the same. This demonstrates how Chinese uses complements rather than prepositions to express directional movement.

items.question: 他把书放_____了书包里。
items.translation: (He put the book into the backpack.)
items.explanation: 放进 (fàng jìn) means 'to put into.' This combines the 把 construction with a direction complement: 把 + Object + Verb + Direction + Location. 书包里 (shūbāo lǐ, inside the backpack) specifies where. The location follows the direction complement. Other examples with 进: 装进 (pack into), 塞进 (stuff into), 倒进 (pour into). This pattern is essential for describing placing objects in containers.

items.question: 天黑了，我们_____吧。
items.explanation: 回去 (huíqù) means 'to go back (away from current speaker position).' 回 (huí, return) + 去 (qù, away from speaker) = go back to another place. Compare: 回来 (huílái) means 'come back (toward speaker).' If someone calls you on the phone, they'd say 你什么时候回来？(When are you coming back?). But if you're leaving, you'd say 我要回去了 (I need to go back). The 来/去 choice depends entirely on perspective.

items.question: 我想不_____他的名字了。
items.explanation: 想不起来 (xiǎng bù qǐlái) means 'cannot recall/remember.' This is a figurative use of 起来 (rise up) — the memory cannot 'rise up' to the surface. The negative potential form inserts 不 between the verb and complement. Positive: 想起来了！(I remembered!/It came to me!) This extended meaning is extremely common. Other figurative uses: 看起来 (looks like/appears), 说起来 (speaking of/when you mention it).

items.question: 请你把灯_____。
items.explanation: 关上 (guān shàng) means 'to close/turn off (completely).' 上 (shàng) here acts as a result-direction complement meaning 'shut/sealed.' It doesn't mean 'up' literally — think of it as 'completing a closing action.' Similarly: 锁上 (lock up), 盖上 (cover up/put the lid on), 合上 (close/fold shut). All use 上 to mean the action of closing or sealing is complete. This is a major extended meaning of 上 that learners must master.

items.question: 这件事你要坚持_____。
items.explanation: 坚持下去 (jiānchí xiàqù) means 'to keep persisting/continue on.' 下去 (xiàqù) has a figurative meaning of 'continuing from now onward.' It expresses the continuation of a state or action into the future: 说下去 (keep talking), 活下去 (keep living), 学下去 (keep studying). This is one of the most important figurative uses of directional complements. It pairs 下 (downward/onward) with 去 (away from now = into the future).

items.question: 他偷偷溜_____了。
items.explanation: 溜出去 (liū chūqù) means 'to slip out/sneak out (away from speaker).' 溜 (liū) means 'to slip/sneak,' and 出去 (chūqù) means 'out and away.' The 去 tells us the person moved away from the speaker's position. Compare: 溜出来 would mean to slip out toward the speaker. The verb 溜 with its sneaky connotation combines naturally with directional complements to create vivid, descriptive language.

items.question: 大家都安静_____了。
items.explanation: 安静下来 (ānjìng xiàlái) means 'to quiet down.' 下来 has a figurative meaning of 'decreasing/settling from a higher state.' It pairs with adjectives and verbs to show calming, decreasing, or settling: 冷静下来 (calm down), 慢下来 (slow down), 停下来 (stop). It suggests movement from an active/intense state to a calmer/lower state. This is the opposite of 起来, which suggests increasing intensity.

items.question: 她哭_____了。
items.explanation: 哭起来 (kū qǐlái) means 'to start crying/burst into tears.' 起来 here indicates the sudden beginning of an action. It's used with emotional or physical reactions: 笑起来 (burst out laughing), 唱起来 (start singing), 跳起来 (start jumping). This 'start doing' meaning of 起来 is one of its most important figurative extensions, showing the onset of an action, especially one that happens suddenly or spontaneously.

name: Degree Complements
description: Using 得 (de) to express the degree or manner of an action's result

items.question: 他跑得很_____。
items.explanation: 跑得很快 (pǎo de hěn kuài) means 'runs very fast.' The degree complement structure uses 得 (de) after the verb to describe HOW the action is performed or what degree it reaches. The pattern is: Verb + 得 + Adjective/Description. This 得 (de) is different from 的 (possessive) and 地 (adverbial). 得 specifically links a verb to its manner or result. This is one of the three essential 'de' particles every Chinese learner must master.

items.question: 她说中文说得很_____。
items.explanation: 说得很好 (shuō de hěn hǎo) means 'speaks very well.' When a verb has an object, the verb must be repeated: 她说中文说得很好. The first 说中文 states what she speaks, and the second 说得很好 describes how well. You CANNOT say 她说中文得很好 — the object cannot come between the verb and 得. This 'verb repetition' rule is one of the trickiest aspects of degree complements for English speakers.

items.question: 昨天我睡得很_____。
items.explanation: 睡得很晚 (shuì de hěn wǎn) means 'slept very late (went to bed late).' 晚 (wǎn, late) describes the degree/manner of sleeping. Note that in English 'slept late' could mean waking up late, but in Chinese 睡得很晚 specifically means going to bed at a late hour. To say 'woke up late,' you'd say 起得很晚 (qǐ de hěn wǎn). The degree complement precisely describes the characteristic of the action.

items.question: 他高兴得跳_____了。
items.translation: (He was so happy he jumped up.)
items.explanation: 高兴得跳起来 (gāoxìng de tiào qǐlái) means 'so happy that (he) jumped up.' Here the degree complement describes the RESULT of an extreme emotion: 'happy to the point of jumping up.' The pattern is: Adjective + 得 + Result Action. This shows that 得 complements aren't just simple descriptions — they can express cause-and-effect: the emotion was SO strong it caused a physical reaction. Other examples: 累得睡着了 (so tired that fell asleep).

items.question: 这个菜做得_____好吃。
items.explanation: 做得很好吃 (zuò de hěn hǎochī) means 'made very deliciously.' 很 (hěn, very) is the most common intensifier in degree complements. The structure Verb + 得 + 很 + Adjective is the standard pattern for expressing a positive degree. Note: in Chinese, 很 is often used as a 'default' intensifier even when you don't mean 'very' — 做得好吃 without 很 can sound incomplete or like a comparison. Adding 很 makes it a neutral positive statement.

items.question: 他写汉字写得_____漂亮。
items.translation: (He writes Chinese characters not very neatly.)
items.explanation: 写得不太漂亮 (xiě de bú tài piàoliàng) means 'doesn't write very neatly.' The negative degree complement uses 不 or 不太 after 得: Verb + 得 + 不/不太 + Adjective. 不太 (bú tài, not very) is a softer negation than 不 alone. Compare: 写得不漂亮 (writes ugly — blunt) vs. 写得不太漂亮 (doesn't write so neatly — polite). Chinese speakers often use 不太 to soften criticism, which is culturally important.

items.question: 她唱歌唱得_____好听了。
items.explanation: 唱得太好听了 (chàng de tài hǎotīng le) means 'sings SO beautifully.' 太...了 (tài...le) is an exclamatory degree expression meaning 'so/too...!' In degree complements: Verb + 得 + 太 + Adj + 了. While 太 can mean 'too much' (negative), with 了 it often expresses admiration or surprise: 太好了 (That's great!), 太漂亮了 (So beautiful!). The 了 at the end adds emotional emphasis.

items.question: 他紧张得说不_____话来。
items.translation: (He was so nervous he couldn't speak.)
items.explanation: 紧张得说不出话来 (jǐnzhāng de shuō bù chū huà lái) means 'so nervous that (he) couldn't get words out.' This combines a degree complement (得) with a potential complement (说不出). The pattern: Emotion + 得 + Potential Complement (negative), showing an extreme emotional state causing inability. 说不出话来 literally means 'words cannot come out.' This is a vivid, commonly used expression that demonstrates advanced complement layering.

items.question: 这本书写得很有_____。
items.explanation: 写得很有意思 (xiě de hěn yǒu yìsi) means 'written very interestingly.' The degree complement can use phrases, not just adjectives: Verb + 得 + 有/没有 + Noun. 有意思 (yǒu yìsi, interesting/fun) is a very common expression. Other phrase-type complements: 做得有道理 (done reasonably), 说得有信心 (spoken with confidence). This shows the flexibility of the 得 complement structure beyond simple adjectives.

items.question: 今天冷得我不想出_____。
items.translation: (Today it's so cold I don't want to go out.)
items.explanation: 冷得不想出门 (lěng de bù xiǎng chūmén) means 'so cold that I don't want to go out.' 出门 (chūmén, go out/leave the house) treats 门 (door) as a bound object. The degree complement here expresses consequence: cold to the degree that going out is undesirable. The pattern Adj + 得 + Consequence Clause is very expressive and natural in Chinese conversation, allowing speakers to dramatize situations vividly.

items.question: 她感动得流了_____。
items.translation: (She was so moved that she shed tears.)
items.explanation: 感动得流了眼泪 (gǎndòng de liú le yǎnlèi) means 'so moved that tears flowed.' 感动 (gǎndòng, moved/touched) + 得 + result clause. 流眼泪 (liú yǎnlèi) means 'to shed tears.' This is an emotionally vivid degree complement — the emotion was so intense it produced a physical response (tears). Chinese frequently uses this cause-effect degree pattern for emotional expressions, making the language very descriptive and evocative.

items.question: 这个学生学得_____认真。
items.explanation: 学得挺认真 (xué de tǐng rènzhēn) means 'studies quite seriously.' 挺 (tǐng) is a colloquial intensifier meaning 'quite/rather,' softer than 很 but still positive. Other intensifiers used in degree complements: 非常 (fēicháng, extremely), 特别 (tèbié, especially), 相当 (xiāngdāng, considerably). Each adds a different shade of intensity. 挺 is casual and conversational, while 非常 is more formal. Choosing the right intensifier shows language sophistication.

items.question: 他们玩得很_____。
items.explanation: 玩得很开心 (wán de hěn kāixīn) means 'played/had fun very happily.' 开心 (kāixīn, happy/joyful) describes the experiential quality of the activity. This is a very natural and frequently used expression. When someone returns from an outing, a common question is 玩得开心吗？(Did you have fun?). The degree complement here evaluates the subjective experience, not just the action itself.

items.question: 他忙得连饭都没_____吃。
items.translation: (He was so busy he didn't even have time to eat.)
items.explanation: 忙得连饭都没来得及吃 (máng de lián fàn dōu méi láidejí chī) means 'so busy that he didn't even have time to eat.' 来得及 (láidejí) means 'to have time to.' 连...都 (lián...dōu) means 'even...all' and emphasizes extremeness. This complex degree complement shows advanced usage: Adj + 得 + 连 + Object + 都 + Negative + Verb. It's a dramatic construction expressing that something was so extreme that even basic activities were affected.

items.question: 这道数学题难得我做了_____个小时。
items.translation: (This math problem was so difficult that I spent two hours on it.)
items.explanation: 难得做了两个小时 (nán de zuò le liǎng gè xiǎoshí) means 'so difficult that it took two hours.' The degree complement here expresses the consequence of difficulty — extended time spent. 两 (liǎng) is used instead of 二 (èr) before measure words. Note: 难得 as two separate words (adjective + 得 complement) is different from 难得 as one word (nándé, rare/seldom). Context and pronunciation distinguish them. This is a common source of confusion for learners.

items.question: 这个字太小了，我看_____。
items.translation: (This character is too small, I can't see it.)
items.explanation: 看不清楚 (kàn bu qīngchu) means 'can't see clearly.' The potential complement uses 不 (bù) between the verb and complement to express inability: V + 不 + complement. This structure indicates the action CANNOT achieve the result. The opposite would be 看得清楚 (can see clearly), using 得 instead of 不. It focuses on ability/possibility, not willingness.

items.question: 这首歌太难了，我唱_____。
items.translation: (This song is too difficult, I can't sing it.)
items.explanation: 唱不好 (chàng bù hǎo) means 'can't sing well.' The potential complement 不好 after 唱 indicates inability to reach the standard of 'good.' This is the negative potential form: V + 不 + complement. It expresses that the action cannot achieve the desired result due to difficulty or inability. Compare: 唱得好 (can sing well) with 得 for positive potential.

items.question: 这个箱子太重了，我搬_____。
items.translation: (This box is too heavy, I can't move it.)
items.explanation: 搬不动 (bān bu dòng) means 'can't move (something).' The complement 动 (dòng, to move) combined with 不 creates the potential complement 'unable to move.' This is common with physical actions where weight or difficulty prevents completion. The pattern V + 不动 is used when physical force is insufficient. Compare: 搬得动 (can move it) for the positive form.

items.question: 这道菜太辣了，我吃_____。
items.translation: (This dish is too spicy, I can't eat it.)
items.explanation: 吃不了 (chī bu liǎo) means 'can't eat (it all/handle it).' The complement 了 (liǎo) in the potential form indicates capacity or tolerance. V + 不了 means the action cannot be completed or tolerated. This is different from the sentence-final 了 (le). 了 as a complement specifically means 'to finish/handle.' Compare: 吃得了 (can eat/handle it).

items.question: 我找了很久，可是_____他。
items.translation: (I looked for a long time, but couldn't find him.)
items.explanation: 找不到 (zhǎo bu dào) means 'can't find.' The complement 到 (dào) means 'to reach/arrive at,' so V + 不到 means the action cannot reach its target or goal. This is one of the most common potential complement patterns in Chinese. Compare: 找得到 (can find). 到 as complement implies successful achievement of the intended result.

items.question: 这么多饭，你吃_____吗？
items.translation: (So much rice, can you finish it?)
items.explanation: 吃得完 (chī de wán) means 'can finish eating.' The complement 完 (wán, to finish) with 得 creates a positive potential: 'able to finish.' The pattern V + 得 + complement asks whether one can complete the action. This question uses 吗 for a yes/no format. The negative would be 吃不完 (can't finish eating). 完 as complement focuses on completion of the entire quantity.

items.question: 这条河太宽了，你游_____吗？
items.translation: (This river is too wide, can you swim across?)
items.explanation: 游得过去 (yóu de guòqu) means 'can swim across.' This is a directional potential complement: V + 得 + directional complement. 过去 (guòqu) means 'to go across/over.' The entire phrase asks if swimming can achieve the result of crossing. Complex directional complements like 过去, 过来, 上去, 下来 can all be used in potential complement patterns with 得/不.

items.question: 你听_____他说什么了吗？
items.explanation: 听得见 (tīng de jiàn) means 'can hear.' The complement 见 (jiàn) literally means 'to see/perceive' but is used with sensory verbs to indicate successful perception. V + 得见 means 'able to perceive through V.' Common pairs: 听得见/听不见 (can/can't hear), 看得见/看不见 (can/can't see). This complement focuses on whether the sensory input reaches the perceiver.

items.question: 这件衣服太小了，我穿_____。
items.translation: (This piece of clothing is too small, I can't wear it.)
items.explanation: 穿不上 (chuān bu shàng) means 'can't put on (clothing).' The directional complement 上 (shàng, up/on) with 不 creates a potential meaning: unable to put ON. This is used when clothing doesn't fit — you physically can't put it on your body. Compare: 穿得上 (can put on) for clothing that fits. The complement 上 implies the action of attaching/donning successfully.

items.question: 你来_____来_____明天的聚会？
items.translation: (Can you come to tomorrow's party or not?)
items.explanation: 来得来不 is a contraction error — the correct full form is V + 得 + C + V + 不 + C, but this simplified question uses the A-not-A pattern for potential complements. 来得了来不了 (lái de liǎo lái bu liǎo) means 'able to come or not.' This is the affirmative-negative question form for potential complements: V得C + V不C, asking whether the action is achievable.

items.question: 门太小了，这个沙发搬_____。
items.translation: (The door is too small, this sofa can't be moved in.)
items.explanation: 搬不进去 (bān bu jìnqu) means 'can't move (it) in.' This is a complex potential complement combining the verb 搬 with the compound directional complement 进去 (jìnqu, to go in). The 不 is inserted between the verb and complement: 搬 + 不 + 进去. The physical obstacle (small door) prevents the successful completion of moving the sofa inside.

items.question: 教室里太吵了，我听_____老师说话。
items.translation: (The classroom is too noisy, I can't hear the teacher speak.)
items.explanation: 听不清 (tīng bu qīng) means 'can't hear clearly.' The complement 清 (qīng, clear) with 不 indicates the inability to clearly perceive sound. This is slightly different from 听不见 (can't hear at all): 听不清 means sound reaches you but isn't clear, while 听不见 means no sound reaches you at all. Both are potential complement patterns but express different degrees.

items.question: 这本书太难了，我看_____。
items.translation: (This book is too difficult, I can't understand it.)
items.explanation: 看不懂 (kàn bu dǒng) means 'can't understand (by reading).' The complement 懂 (dǒng, to understand) combined with 不 expresses the inability to comprehend. This is one of the most useful potential complement patterns: V + 不懂 = can't understand through that action. Similarly: 听不懂 (can't understand by listening), 学不懂 (can't understand by studying). Compare: 看得懂 (can understand).

items.question: 这个东西太贵了，我买_____。
items.translation: (This thing is too expensive, I can't afford it.)
items.explanation: 买不起 (mǎi bu qǐ) means 'can't afford to buy.' The complement 起 (qǐ) in potential form means 'to afford' or 'to be worthy of.' V + 不起 is a very common pattern meaning 'cannot afford (the action).' It implies financial or resource limitations. Other examples: 住不起 (can't afford to live), 吃不起 (can't afford to eat). Compare: 买得起 (can afford).

items.question: 他说得太快了，我记_____。
items.translation: (He spoke too fast, I can't remember it all.)
items.explanation: 记不住 (jì bu zhù) means 'can't remember/retain.' The complement 住 (zhù, to hold/stay) with 不 indicates the inability to 'hold onto' something mentally. V + 不住 means the result of the action can't be maintained or retained. Common examples: 记不住 (can't remember), 站不住 (can't stand steadily), 拿不住 (can't hold onto). Compare: 记得住 (can remember).

items.question: 我学了三_____中文了。
items.translation: (I have been studying Chinese for three years.)
items.explanation: 三年 (sān nián) means 'three years.' Duration in Chinese is placed AFTER the verb: V + 了 + duration. The pattern 学了三年 means 'have studied for three years.' The second 了 at the end of the sentence indicates the action continues to the present. When a verb takes both an object and duration, the order is: V + 了 + duration + 的 + object, or V + object + V + 了 + duration.

items.question: 他在北京住了两_____。
items.translation: (He lived in Beijing for two months.)
items.explanation: 两个月 (liǎng ge yuè) means 'two months.' Note that 月 (month) requires the measure word 个 when expressing duration: 几个月, 两个月. This is different from 年 (year) and 天 (day) which don't need measure words. Also note 两 (liǎng) is used instead of 二 (èr) before measure words. The duration 两个月 follows the verb 住了 to show how long the action lasted.

items.question: 请等_____，我马上来。
items.translation: (Please wait a moment, I'll come right away.)
items.explanation: 等一下 (děng yīxià) means 'wait a moment.' 一下 (yīxià) after a verb indicates a brief action or 'doing something a little bit.' It softens the tone and makes requests more polite. Compare with 一会儿 (yīhuìr), which means 'for a while' — a slightly longer duration. 一下 is commonly used in daily conversation to express quick, brief actions: 看一下 (take a look), 说一下 (say a bit).

items.question: 我们已经走了一个_____了。
items.translation: (We have already walked for one hour.)
items.explanation: 一个小时 (yī ge xiǎoshí) means 'one hour.' When expressing duration in hours, use 个 as the measure word: 一个小时, 两个小时, 半个小时. The structure V + 了 + duration + 了 indicates the action started in the past and continues. 钟头 (zhōngtóu) is a colloquial alternative for 'hour,' but 小时 is standard. Note: 点 is for telling time (3点 = 3 o'clock), not duration.

items.question: 她每天跑步跑_____分钟。
items.translation: (She runs for thirty minutes every day.)
items.explanation: 三十分钟 (sānshí fēnzhōng) means 'thirty minutes.' When the verb takes an object that is the same as the verb (跑步/跑), the pattern repeats the verb: 跑步跑三十分钟. This avoids ambiguity about whether the duration modifies the verb or the object. Alternatively, you can say 跑三十分钟的步. This verb-copying structure is essential for duration with verb-object compounds.

items.question: 我等了你_____了！
items.translation: (I waited for you for half a day!)
items.explanation: 半天 (bàntiān) literally means 'half a day' but is commonly used idiomatically to mean 'a long time' (often with mild complaint). The structure V + 了 + person + duration shows how long one waited for someone. 半天 is a very natural expression in spoken Chinese, often conveying impatience: 想了半天 (thought for ages), 找了半天 (searched forever). It's more colloquial than literal.

items.question: 他睡了整整一_____。
items.explanation: 一夜 (yī yè) means 'one night (duration).' 整整 (zhěngzhěng) means 'a whole/entire' and emphasizes completeness. 夜 (yè) is used for nighttime duration, while 晚 (wǎn) means 'evening' and isn't typically used as a duration counter. Compare: 一天一夜 (one day and one night), 三天两夜 (three days and two nights). The verb 睡 (sleep) naturally pairs with nighttime durations.

items.question: 从北京到上海坐高铁要四个_____。
items.translation: (Taking the high-speed train from Beijing to Shanghai takes four hours.)
items.explanation: 四个小时 (sì ge xiǎoshí) means 'four hours.' The construction 要 + duration means 'it takes (amount of time).' The full pattern: 从A到B + transport + 要 + duration expresses travel time. 小时 (xiǎoshí) is the standard word for 'hour' in duration expressions. Note: 要 here means 'requires/needs,' not 'want.' This is a crucial distinction in Chinese where 要 has multiple meanings depending on context.

items.question: 她学钢琴学了_____多年了。
items.translation: (She has been learning piano for more than ten years.)
items.explanation: 十多年 (shí duō nián) means 'more than ten years.' The word 多 (duō) after a number means 'more than/over' that amount. For round numbers (十, 百, 千), 多 comes right after: 十多年, 一百多块. For non-round numbers, 多 comes after the measure word: 三年多 (a little over 3 years). This is a key pattern distinction: 十多 = 10+ (could be 11-19), while 三年多 = 3 years and some months.

items.question: 他们认识才_____个星期。
items.translation: (They've only known each other for two weeks.)
items.explanation: 两个星期 (liǎng ge xīngqī) means 'two weeks.' 才 (cái) here emphasizes that the duration is SHORT — 'only/merely.' This contrasts with 就 (jiù) which would imply the time is surprisingly long or early. 才两个星期 = 'only two weeks (unexpectedly short).' The 才/就 distinction is important for expressing the speaker's attitude toward the duration: 才 implies 'less than expected,' 就 implies 'more/sooner than expected.'

items.question: 这部电影有两个小时_____。
items.translation: (This movie is two and a half hours long.)
items.explanation: 两个小时半 (liǎng ge xiǎoshí bàn) means 'two and a half hours.' 半 (bàn, half) is placed after the time unit to add half: 一年半 (1.5 years), 三天半 (3.5 days), 一个月半 (1.5 months). This is different from putting 半 before: 半个小时 (half an hour). The pattern is: number + unit + 半 = number.5 units. 有...长 or 有... means 'is (duration) long.'

items.question: 我在这家公司工作了差不多_____年。
items.translation: (I have worked at this company for almost five years.)
items.explanation: 差不多五年 (chàbuduō wǔ nián) means 'almost five years.' 差不多 (chàbuduō) means 'almost/approximately' and is placed BEFORE the number to indicate the actual duration is close to but hasn't reached that amount. Compare with 左右 (zuǒyòu, approximately), which goes AFTER: 五年左右 (around 5 years, could be more or less). 差不多 implies 'not quite there yet,' while 左右 is more neutral.

items.question: 他出国_____了，下个月才回来。
items.translation: (He has been abroad for a long time, he won't come back until next month.)
items.explanation: 很久 (hěn jiǔ) means 'a long time.' 久 (jiǔ) specifically refers to duration of time (how long), while 长 (cháng) refers to physical length. In time expressions, 久 is standard: 很久以前 (a long time ago), 等了很久 (waited for a long time). 才 in the second clause means 'not until' — emphasizing the late return. The combination of 很久 and 才 creates a sense of long absence.

items.question: 我每天早上锻炼_____个小时。
items.translation: (I exercise for one and a half hours every morning.)
items.explanation: 一个半小时 (yī ge bàn xiǎoshí) means 'one and a half hours.' When 小时 is used, 半 goes between 个 and 小时: 一个半小时. This is slightly different from simpler units: 一年半 (1.5 years), 一天半 (1.5 days) where 半 goes directly after the unit. With 个 as measure word, the structure is: number + 个半 + noun. This is a fixed pattern unique to measure-word phrases.

items.question: 从他毕业到现在已经过了_____年了。
items.translation: (It has already been ten years since he graduated.)
items.explanation: 十年 (shí nián) means 'ten years.' The structure 从...到现在已经过了...年了 means 'from (event) until now, (X) years have already passed.' 已经 (yǐjīng, already) emphasizes completion, and 过了 (guòle) means 'have passed.' The final 了 indicates the current state. This is a formal way to express elapsed time from a specific starting point. It combines multiple time-marking elements into one comprehensive statement.

items.question: 我每天都_____八杯水。
items.translation: (I drink eight glasses of water every day.)
items.explanation: 每天都喝 (měi tiān dōu hē) means 'drink every day.' 每 (měi) means 'every/each' and pairs with 都 (dōu, all) for emphasis: 每...都... This is the standard frequency pattern. 每天 (every day), 每周 (every week), 每年 (every year). 都 reinforces that the action happens WITHOUT EXCEPTION. While 都 can be omitted, its presence strengthens the universal meaning: 'every single day without fail.'

items.question: 他一个星期去_____次健身房。
items.translation: (He goes to the gym three times a week.)
items.explanation: 三次 (sān cì) means 'three times.' The frequency pattern in Chinese is: time period + V + number + 次. 次 (cì) is the measure word for frequency/occurrences. It's placed AFTER the verb (unlike English 'three times a week'): 一个星期去三次 = 'one week go three times.' This word order is essential — the frequency count always follows the verb in Chinese grammar.

items.question: 她_____去看望父母一次。
items.translation: (She visits her parents once a month.)
items.explanation: 每个月 (měi ge yuè) means 'every month.' Note that 月 requires the measure word 个: 每个月 (not 每月, though 每月 is acceptable in formal writing). Compare: 每天 (every day — no measure word needed), 每年 (every year — no measure word), 每个星期 (every week — uses 个). The full pattern 每个月...一次 means 'once every month.'

items.question: 我们很少_____那家餐厅吃饭。
items.explanation: 很少去 (hěn shǎo qù) means 'rarely go.' 很少 (hěn shǎo) means 'rarely/seldom' and is a frequency adverb placed before the verb. Chinese frequency adverbs from most to least: 总是/一直 (always) > 经常/常常 (often) > 有时候 (sometimes) > 偶尔 (occasionally) > 很少 (rarely) > 从不/从来不 (never). These adverbs go before the verb, after the subject.

items.question: 他_____迟到，老板很不高兴。
items.translation: (He is always late, the boss is very unhappy.)
items.explanation: 总是 (zǒngshì) means 'always.' It indicates an action happens 100% of the time and often carries a tone of complaint or emphasis. Compare with 一直 (yīzhí), which means 'continuously/all along' without the complaining nuance. In this sentence, 总是迟到 implies the boss has reason to be upset because it happens repeatedly without change. 总是 is stronger than 经常 (often).

items.question: 她_____周末去图书馆学习。
items.translation: (She often goes to the library to study on weekends.)
items.explanation: 经常 (jīngcháng) means 'often/frequently.' It indicates a high but not absolute frequency — roughly 70-80% of the time. Synonyms include 常常 (chángcháng) and 时常 (shícháng). 经常 is more common in spoken Chinese. Position: subject + 经常 + verb. Unlike 总是 (always, 100%), 经常 acknowledges that the action doesn't happen every single time but is a regular habit.

items.question: 我_____吃辣的东西，大概一年一两次。
items.translation: (I occasionally eat spicy food, about once or twice a year.)
items.explanation: 偶尔 (ǒu'ěr) means 'occasionally/once in a while.' It indicates a very low frequency — something that happens rarely and irregularly. The second clause 大概一年一两次 (roughly once or twice a year) clarifies just how infrequent. 偶尔 sits between 很少 (rarely, suggesting almost never) and 有时候 (sometimes, more frequent). 偶尔 often implies the action is not part of one's regular routine.

items.question: 一般来说，我_____坐公交车上班。
items.translation: (Generally speaking, I usually take the bus to work.)
items.explanation: 通常 (tōngcháng) means 'usually/normally.' It indicates what happens in the typical or standard case — around 80-90% of the time. 一般来说 (yībān lái shuō) means 'generally speaking' and often pairs with 通常. Similar words: 一般 (yībān, generally), 平时 (píngshí, normally/on regular days). 通常 is slightly more formal than 经常 and emphasizes what is 'normal' rather than just 'frequent.'

items.question: 我从来_____说过这样的话。
items.explanation: 从来没 (cónglái méi) means 'have never.' 从来 (cónglái) means 'ever/from the beginning' and must be followed by a negative: 从来不 (never do — for habitual actions) or 从来没 (have never — for past experiences). Here, 从来没...过 means 'have never (done something).' The distinction is: 从来不吃肉 (never eat meat — habit), 从来没吃过 (have never eaten — experience). The 过 reinforces the experiential aspect.

items.question: 他_____来这里一趟。
items.translation: (He comes here once every few days.)
items.explanation: 隔几天 (gé jǐ tiān) means 'every few days/at intervals of several days.' 隔 (gé) means 'at intervals of' and creates a specific frequency pattern: 隔 + number + time unit = 'every X (time units).' Examples: 隔一天 (every other day), 隔两个星期 (every two weeks). 一趟 (yī tàng) means 'one trip/visit.' This pattern is more precise than 经常 because it specifies the interval between occurrences.

items.question: 这种事_____发生。
items.translation: (This kind of thing happens from time to time.)
items.explanation: 时不时 (shí bu shí) means 'from time to time/now and then.' It's a colloquial expression indicating irregular, unpredictable frequency. Similar expressions: 时而 (shí'ér, now and then — more literary), 不时 (bùshí, occasionally). 时不时 suggests something pops up intermittently without a fixed schedule. It's commonly used in spoken Chinese and has a natural, conversational tone. Position: before the verb.

items.question: 他一天抽两_____烟。
items.translation: (He smokes two packs of cigarettes a day.)
items.explanation: 两包烟 (liǎng bāo yān) means 'two packs of cigarettes.' This sentence demonstrates frequency with quantity: time period + V + quantity. 包 (bāo) is the measure word for 'pack/package.' Different cigarette measure words: 包 (pack), 根/支 (individual cigarette), 条 (carton). The structure 一天...两包 means 'two packs per day,' showing frequency through the time-frame + quantity pattern.

items.question: 我们_____见一次面。
items.translation: (We meet once every half a year.)
items.explanation: 半年见一次面 (bàn nián jiàn yī cì miàn) means 'meet once every six months.' 半年 (bàn nián) means 'half a year.' The pattern time period + V + number + 次 expresses how often something occurs within that timeframe. 见面 is a verb-object compound (separable verb): 见一次面, not 见面一次. When adding frequency to separable verbs, the frequency expression goes between the verb and object parts.

items.question: 她_____给妈妈打电话。
items.translation: (She calls her mom every day without fail.)
items.explanation: 天天 (tiāntiān) means 'every day/daily.' It's the colloquial, emphatic version of 每天 (měitiān). 天天 implies consistency and routine — 'day after day without missing.' While 每天 is neutral ('every day'), 天天 carries emotional weight: either admiration (天天锻炼, exercises daily — good habit) or complaint (天天加班, works overtime daily — frustration). Other doubled time words: 年年 (every year), 月月 (every month).

items.question: 这个药要_____八小时吃一次。
items.translation: (This medicine should be taken once every eight hours.)
items.explanation: 每隔八小时 (měi gé bā xiǎoshí) means 'every eight hours.' 每隔 (měi gé) combines 每 (every) with 隔 (at intervals of) for precise frequency: 每隔 + time interval + V + 一次. This is more precise than simply 每: 每隔两天 (every two days) vs 每两天 (every two days — same meaning but 每隔 is clearer). This pattern is especially common in medical instructions and schedules where exact intervals matter.

items.question: 桌子上_____一本书。
items.translation: (There is a book on the table.)
items.explanation: 桌子上有 (zhuōzi shàng yǒu) means 'on the table there is.' The existential 有 pattern in Chinese is: Place + 有 + indefinite object. This states that something EXISTS at a location. The subject is the PLACE (桌子上), not the object. This is the opposite of English word order. Key rule: the object after 有 must be INDEFINITE (一本书, not 那本书). For definite objects, use 在: 书在桌子上 (The book is on the table).

items.question: 教室里_____三十个学生。
items.translation: (There are thirty students in the classroom.)
items.explanation: 教室里有 (jiàoshì lǐ yǒu) means 'in the classroom there are.' This follows the existential pattern: Location + 有 + quantity + noun. When expressing how many of something exist somewhere, 有 is the standard verb. The location word 里 (lǐ, inside) makes 教室 (classroom) into a location phrase. All Chinese existential sentences put the location FIRST, followed by the verb 有, then the thing that exists.

items.question: 门口_____着一辆车。
items.translation: (There is a car parked at the entrance.)
items.explanation: 门口停着一辆车 (ménkǒu tíng zhe yī liàng chē) means 'a car is parked at the entrance.' This is the V + 着 existential pattern: Place + V + 着 + object. Unlike the 有 pattern, this describes HOW something exists at a location (parked, hung, placed). The 着 (zhe) indicates a continuing state. Common verbs in this pattern: 放着 (placed), 挂着 (hanging), 坐着 (sitting), 贴着 (pasted). The verb describes the posture or manner of existence.

items.question: 花园里_____了很多花。
items.translation: (Many flowers have bloomed in the garden.)
items.explanation: 花园里开了很多花 (huāyuán lǐ kāi le hěn duō huā) means 'many flowers have bloomed in the garden.' This is the V + 了 existential pattern: Place + V + 了 + object. It describes a CHANGE in state — flowers weren't there before, now they are (they bloomed). Compare with V + 着 (continuing state): 花园里开着很多花 (flowers are in bloom). The 了 version emphasizes the emergence or appearance, while 着 emphasizes the ongoing state.

items.question: 墙上_____着一幅画。
items.translation: (There is a painting hanging on the wall.)
items.explanation: 墙上挂着一幅画 (qiáng shàng guà zhe yī fú huà) means 'a painting is hanging on the wall.' The V + 着 existential sentence uses specific posture verbs. 挂 (guà, to hang) tells us HOW the painting exists on the wall — it's hanging, not leaning or lying. Measure word: 幅 (fú) is specifically for paintings, scrolls, and framed artwork. This pattern creates vivid descriptions by specifying the exact manner of the object's placement.

items.question: 这个城市_____一千多万人口。
items.translation: (This city has a population of over ten million.)
items.explanation: 有一千多万人口 (yǒu yīqiān duō wàn rénkǒu) means 'has a population of over ten million.' 有 in existential sentences can express abstract possession/existence too, not just physical location. Large numbers in Chinese: 千 (thousand), 万 (ten thousand), 百万 (million), 千万 (ten million), 亿 (hundred million). 一千多万 = 10 million plus. This is the abstract existential: Subject + 有 + quantity.

items.question: 前面_____一家超市。
items.explanation: 前面有 (qiánmiàn yǒu) means 'ahead there is.' Directional location words in Chinese: 前面 (ahead), 后面 (behind), 左边 (left), 右边 (right), 上面 (above), 下面 (below), 旁边 (beside), 对面 (opposite), 附近 (nearby). These serve as the location subject in existential sentences: Direction + 有 + object. This pattern is extremely useful for giving directions and describing spatial relationships.

items.question: 这里_____人吗？
items.explanation: 这里有人吗 (zhèlǐ yǒu rén ma) means 'Is there anyone here?' This is the question form of the existential sentence. To negate, use 没有: 这里没有人 (there's no one here). The question can also be formed with 有没有: 这里有没有人? 有 in existential questions checks for existence: 有没有问题? (Are there any questions?). Note: 有 is ALWAYS negated with 没, never with 不.

items.question: 冰箱里什么都_____。
items.explanation: 什么都没有 (shénme dōu méiyǒu) means 'there is nothing at all.' The pattern 什么都 + negative means 'nothing whatsoever.' 都 (dōu) here intensifies the universal negative — absolutely nothing. This is stronger than just 没有东西 (there are no things). Other interrogative + 都 + negative patterns: 谁都没来 (nobody came), 哪里都没去 (didn't go anywhere). The interrogative word becomes a universal negative with 都.

items.question: 操场上_____着几个孩子在玩。
items.translation: (There are several children playing on the playground.)
items.explanation: 操场上跑着几个孩子 (cāochǎng shàng pǎo zhe jǐ ge háizi) means 'several children are running on the playground.' This V + 着 existential sentence uses 跑 (pǎo, to run) to describe the state — children exist on the playground in a state of running. The 在玩 adds a second layer: while running, they're playing. This creates a dynamic scene description, combining existential structure with manner of activity.

items.question: 天上_____了乌云。
items.translation: (Dark clouds have appeared in the sky.)
items.explanation: 天上出现了乌云 (tiānshàng chūxiàn le wūyún) means 'dark clouds have appeared in the sky.' 出现 (chūxiàn, to appear) is an emergence verb used in existential sentences: Place + emergence verb + 了 + subject. This pattern describes something new coming into existence at a location. Other emergence verbs: 来了 (came), 出来了 (came out), 飞来了 (flew over). The 了 marks the change from non-existence to existence.

items.question: 书架上_____着各种各样的书。
items.translation: (Various kinds of books are arranged on the bookshelf.)
items.explanation: 书架上摆着 (shūjià shàng bǎi zhe) means 'arranged/displayed on the bookshelf.' 摆 (bǎi) means 'to arrange/display,' describing how the books exist on the shelf — neatly arranged, not just randomly placed. 各种各样 (gè zhǒng gè yàng) means 'all kinds of/various.' The V + 着 existential pattern with specific verbs like 摆 creates more descriptive, vivid sentences than the simple 有 pattern.

items.question: 这个学校_____五千多名学生。
items.translation: (This school has over five thousand students.)
items.explanation: 有五千多名学生 (yǒu wǔqiān duō míng xuéshēng) means 'has over five thousand students.' 名 (míng) is a formal measure word for people, more respectful than 个 (ge). It's used in official contexts: 学生, 员工, 运动员. 五千多 means 'over 5,000.' This is an abstract existential expressing what a school 'possesses' in terms of enrollment. The pattern Subject + 有 + number + measure word + noun is used for institutional statistics.

items.question: 路边_____着一棵老树。
items.translation: (An old tree stands by the roadside.)
items.explanation: 路边长着 (lùbiān zhǎng zhe) means 'growing by the roadside.' 长 (zhǎng, to grow) as a posture verb in the existential pattern describes how the tree exists — by growing. 棵 (kē) is the measure word for trees and plants. The V + 着 pattern here creates a scenic description: the tree's existence is characterized by its growth. Other nature-themed existential verbs: 流着 (flowing — rivers), 开着 (blooming — flowers), 飘着 (floating — clouds).

items.question: 会议室里_____着一台投影仪。
items.translation: (There is a projector set up in the meeting room.)
items.explanation: 会议室里放着 (huìyìshì lǐ fàng zhe) means 'placed in the meeting room.' 放 (fàng, to place/put) describes how the projector exists — it was placed there intentionally. 台 (tái) is the measure word for machines and equipment (computers, TVs, projectors). The V + 着 existential sentence conveys not just existence but also human arrangement — someone put the projector there. This adds a layer of meaning beyond simple 有 (there is).

items.question: 我去商店_____东西。
items.translation: (I go to the store to buy things.)
items.explanation: 去商店买东西 (qù shāngdiàn mǎi dōngxi) means 'go to the store to buy things.' This is a serial verb construction: Subject + V1 + V2. The actions happen in sequence — first GO, then BUY. In Chinese, no connecting word is needed between the verbs (unlike English 'to'). The first verb often indicates movement (去, 来, 回), and the second verb states the purpose. This is one of the most fundamental Chinese sentence patterns.

items.question: 她坐公交车_____上班。
items.translation: (She takes the bus to go to work.)
items.explanation: 坐公交车去上班 (zuò gōngjiāochē qù shàngbān) means 'take the bus to go to work.' This three-verb serial construction shows: means of transport (坐公交车, ride bus) + directional movement (去, go) + purpose (上班, work). The verbs chain naturally in chronological order: take bus → go → work. No prepositions or conjunctions are needed. This pattern extends to any transportation: 骑自行车去学校 (ride bike to school), 开车去机场 (drive to airport).

items.question: 请用中文_____这个问题。
items.explanation: 用中文回答 (yòng zhōngwén huídá) means 'use Chinese to answer.' The verb 用 (yòng, to use) creates an instrumental serial verb construction: 用 + tool/means + V2. The first verb specifies the instrument or method, and the second verb states the main action. Other examples: 用筷子吃饭 (use chopsticks to eat), 用电脑写报告 (use a computer to write a report). 用 serial verbs are extremely common in daily Chinese.

items.question: 他拿了一本书_____起来。
items.translation: (He picked up a book and started reading.)
items.explanation: 拿了一本书看起来 (ná le yī běn shū kàn qǐlái) means 'picked up a book and started reading.' The serial verb pattern here shows sequential actions: 拿 (pick up) → 看 (read). 起来 (qǐlái) after the second verb means 'start doing.' This construction narrates a sequence of connected actions. The first verb is often a preparatory physical action, and the second is the intended activity. No conjunction is needed between the two verbs.

items.question: 妈妈去市场买菜_____饭。
items.translation: (Mom goes to the market to buy groceries and cook.)
items.explanation: 买菜做饭 (mǎi cài zuò fàn) means 'buy groceries and cook.' This extends the serial verb chain: 去 (go) + 买菜 (buy groceries) + 做饭 (cook). Each verb follows naturally in time sequence. In Chinese, when actions are logically connected steps, they chain without any conjunction. This mirrors how actions actually unfold: go → buy → cook. The chain can extend further: 去市场买菜回家做饭 (go to market, buy groceries, return home, cook).

items.question: 他站_____说话。
items.explanation: 站起来说话 (zhàn qǐlái shuōhuà) means 'stood up to speak.' The serial verb construction here is: posture change (站起来, stand up) + action (说话, speak). 起来 as a directional complement means 'up/rising.' The physical movement precedes and enables the communicative action. This pattern is common with posture verbs: 坐下来休息 (sit down to rest), 走过来帮忙 (walk over to help). The first verb establishes physical position for the second action.

items.question: 我带了雨伞_____下雨。
items.translation: (I brought an umbrella in case it rains.)
items.explanation: 带了雨伞防下雨 (dài le yǔsǎn fáng xiàyǔ) means 'brought an umbrella to prevent (getting wet in) rain.' This is a purpose-type serial verb construction: action (bring umbrella) + purpose (guard against rain). 防 (fáng) means 'to guard against/prevent.' Serial verbs can express purpose without adding 为了 (in order to), though 为了 is also common: 为了防下雨带了雨伞. The streamlined version without 为了 is more natural in spoken Chinese.

items.question: 老师让学生_____看书。
items.translation: (The teacher has the students sit down and read.)
items.explanation: 让学生坐下看书 (ràng xuéshēng zuò xia kànshū) means 'have the students sit down and read.' This combines a pivotal construction (让 + person) with serial verbs (坐下 + 看书). 让 introduces the person being directed, and their subsequent actions chain in serial verb format. The student first sits down, then reads. This layered structure is common in instructions: 叫他进来坐下等一会儿 (tell him to come in, sit down, and wait a moment).

items.question: 孩子们跑出去_____了。
items.explanation: 跑出去玩 (pǎo chūqù wán) means 'ran out to play.' The serial construction is: movement verb with direction (跑出去, run out) + purpose verb (玩, play). 出去 (chūqù, to go out) is a compound directional complement. The entire phrase shows: manner of movement (running) + direction (outward) + purpose (playing). This three-layer serial verb is natural and efficient: three verbs, three pieces of information, no conjunctions needed.

items.question: 我打电话_____他明天来。
items.translation: (I'll call and tell him to come tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 打电话告诉他 (dǎ diànhuà gàosu tā) means 'call and tell him.' This serial verb shows: method (打电话, make a phone call) + communicative action (告诉, tell). The second verb 告诉 takes its own object clause (他明天来, him to come tomorrow). Serial verbs can nest complex structures — the second verb here introduces a pivot structure where 他 is both the object of 告诉 and the subject of 来. This layering is natural in Chinese.

items.question: 她穿了一件红裙子_____参加婚礼。
items.translation: (She wore a red dress to attend the wedding.)
items.explanation: 穿了一件红裙子去参加婚礼 (chuān le yī jiàn hóng qúnzi qù cānjiā hūnlǐ) means 'wore a red dress to go attend the wedding.' This multi-verb chain shows: preparation (穿, wore dress) + movement (去, went) + event (参加, attended). Each verb adds to the narrative in chronological order. The serial verb construction here tells a mini-story: getting dressed → going → attending. Chinese efficiently chains these without conjunctions that English requires ('and,' 'to').

items.question: 我们开车_____那个小镇。
items.explanation: 开车去 (kāichē qù) means 'drive to/go by car.' The serial verb pattern is: means of transport (开车, drive) + directional verb (去, go) + destination. 开车 is a verb-object compound meaning 'drive a car.' Other transport serial verbs: 坐飞机去 (fly to), 骑马去 (ride a horse to), 走路去 (walk to). The transport means always comes first, establishing HOW the movement happens before specifying WHERE.

items.question: 他回家_____晚饭。
items.explanation: 回家吃晚饭 (huí jiā chī wǎnfàn) means 'go home to eat dinner.' The serial verb construction: 回家 (return home) + 吃晚饭 (eat dinner). The first verb phrase indicates movement (going home), and the second indicates purpose (eating). This is perhaps the most common daily serial verb pattern in Chinese. Similar patterns: 回家休息 (go home to rest), 回家睡觉 (go home to sleep). No preposition 'to' is needed — the purpose follows movement directly.

items.question: 请排队_____票。
items.explanation: 排队买票 (páiduì mǎi piào) means 'line up to buy tickets.' Serial verb pattern: prerequisite action (排队, queue up) + main action (买票, buy tickets). The first verb describes the necessary condition before the second action can occur. This type of serial verb implies that V1 must happen BEFORE V2: you queue first, then buy. Other examples: 洗手吃饭 (wash hands then eat), 签名领奖 (sign name then collect prize).

items.question: 我借了她的车_____机场。
items.translation: (I borrowed her car to go to the airport.)
items.explanation: 借了她的车去机场 (jiè le tā de chē qù jīchǎng) means 'borrowed her car to go to the airport.' This serial verb construction chains: obtaining means (借车, borrow car) + movement (去, go) + destination (机场, airport). The 了 after 借 indicates the borrowing was completed before going. Serial verbs can mark different temporal stages with 了: the first action is completed, enabling the second. This temporal layering adds narrative depth to the serial verb pattern.

items.question: 老师让学生_____作业。
items.translation: (The teacher asks the students to do homework.)
items.explanation: In Chinese pivotal sentences (兼语句, jiānyǔjù), the object of the first verb becomes the subject of the second verb. Here: 老师(S1) + 让(V1) + 学生(O1/S2) + 做(V2) + 作业(O2). The word 让 (ràng) means 'to let/ask someone to do something.' The student is both the object of 让 and the subject of 做. This structure is very common for expressing commands, requests, or permission.

items.question: 妈妈叫我_____去买菜。
items.translation: (Mom told me to go buy groceries.)
items.explanation: 叫 (jiào) means 'to tell/ask someone to do something' in pivotal sentences. Here 妈妈(S1) + 叫(V1) + 我(O1/S2) + 快去买菜(V2 phrase). The adverb 快 (kuài, quickly) modifies the second action. Pivotal verbs like 叫, 让, 请, 使 all follow the same pattern: S1 + V1 + Person + V2.

items.question: 请你_____我介绍一下。
items.explanation: 请 (qǐng) is a polite pivotal verb meaning 'please ask someone to.' Here 请(V1) + 你(O1/S2) + 给我介绍(V2 phrase). The word 给 (gěi) means 'to/for' and indicates the beneficiary. In pivotal constructions with 请, the tone is polite and formal — commonly used in service contexts and introductions.

items.question: 他使我_____非常感动。
items.explanation: 使 (shǐ) means 'to make/cause someone to feel' and creates a pivotal sentence with an emotional result. 他(S1) + 使(V1) + 我(O1/S2) + 感到(V2) + 非常感动. 感到 (gǎndào) means 'to feel.' Unlike 让 or 叫, the verb 使 is more literary and formal, commonly appearing in written Chinese and speeches.

items.question: 我请朋友_____晚饭。
items.translation: (I invited my friend to eat dinner.)
items.explanation: 请 (qǐng) here means 'to invite/treat someone to.' 我(S1) + 请(V1) + 朋友(O1/S2) + 吃(V2) + 晚饭(O2). The friend is both the object being invited and the subject who eats. 请客 (qǐngkè) means 'to treat someone to a meal' — this is a very common social concept in Chinese culture where paying for friends' meals is expected.

items.question: 妈妈不让我_____电脑游戏。
items.translation: (Mom doesn't let me play computer games.)
items.explanation: To negate a pivotal sentence, place 不 before the first verb: 妈妈 + 不让(neg V1) + 我(O1/S2) + 玩(V2) + 电脑游戏(O2). 玩 (wán) means 'to play.' Notice that 不 negates 让 (the permission), not 玩 (the action). This is an important distinction — the negation always targets the first verb in pivotal constructions.

items.question: 老板派他_____出差。
items.translation: (The boss sent him on a business trip.)
items.explanation: 派 (pài) means 'to send/dispatch someone to do something.' 老板(S1) + 派(V1) + 他(O1/S2) + 去(V2) + 出差(O2). The verb 去 (qù, to go) is the second action. 出差 (chūchāi) means 'business trip.' Pivotal verbs like 派 are very common in workplace contexts, showing authority relationships.

items.question: 医生建议他_____休息。
items.translation: (The doctor suggests he should rest more.)
items.explanation: 建议 (jiànyì) means 'to suggest/recommend' and creates a pivotal sentence. 医生(S1) + 建议(V1) + 他(O1/S2) + 多休息(V2). 多 (duō) here is an adverb meaning 'more.' Medical advice in Chinese often uses pivotal constructions with 建议, 劝 (quàn, advise), or 要求 (yāoqiú, require).

items.question: 我叫他_____等我。
items.translation: (I told him to wait for me here.)
items.explanation: 叫(V1) + 他(O1/S2) + 在这儿等我(V2 phrase with location). 在这儿 (zài zhèr) means 'here/at this place' and acts as a location phrase for the second verb. In pivotal sentences, the second verb phrase can be complex, including location words, time words, and adverbs — they all modify the second action, not the first.

items.question: 这个消息令人_____。
items.explanation: 令 (lìng) is a formal pivotal verb meaning 'to cause/make.' 这个消息(S1) + 令(V1) + 人(O1/S2) + 吃惊(V2). 令人 (lìngrén) is a common written expression meaning 'to make people feel.' 吃惊 (chījīng) means 'surprised/shocked.' This pattern is very common in news headlines and formal writing: 令人 + emotion word.

items.question: 父母希望孩子_____好好学习。
items.translation: (Parents hope their children can study hard.)
items.explanation: 希望 (xīwàng) means 'to hope' and creates a pivotal sentence. 父母(S1) + 希望(V1) + 孩子(O1/S2) + 能好好学习(V2 phrase with modal). 能 (néng) adds possibility to the second action. The child is both what the parents hope about and who studies. Note: 好好 (hǎohǎo) means 'properly/well' and is a reduplicated adverb.

items.question: 他邀请我们_____参加他的生日聚会。
items.translation: (He invited us to his birthday party.)
items.explanation: 邀请 (yāoqǐng) means 'to invite.' 他(S1) + 邀请(V1) + 我们(O1/S2) + 去参加(V2) + 他的生日聚会(O2). 去 indicates movement toward the party location. 参加 (cānjiā) means 'to attend/participate.' The combination 去参加 is itself a serial verb construction nested within the pivotal sentence — showing how Chinese structures can layer.

items.question: 这首歌让我_____了很多回忆。
items.translation: (This song reminded me of many memories.)
items.explanation: 让(V1) + 我(O1/S2) + 想起了(V2) + 很多回忆(O2). 想起 (xiǎngqǐ) means 'to recall/think of.' The result complement 起 indicates the memories 'come up.' 了 indicates completion. This emotional use of 让 is extremely common: 让我想起 (makes me recall), 让我感动 (makes me moved), 让我开心 (makes me happy).

items.question: 公司要求员工_____加班。
items.translation: (The company requires employees to work overtime.)
items.explanation: 要求 (yāoqiú) means 'to require/demand' — a strong pivotal verb. 公司(S1) + 要求(V1) + 员工(O1/S2) + 每天加班(V2 phrase). 每天 (měitiān) means 'every day' and modifies the second action 加班 (jiābān, to work overtime). The pivotal verb 要求 is more forceful than 让 or 请, implying obligation.

items.question: 我劝他_____抽烟。
items.explanation: 劝 (quàn) means 'to advise/persuade.' 我(S1) + 劝(V1) + 他(O1/S2) + 别抽烟(V2 phrase). 别 (bié) is used for negative commands/advice meaning 'don't.' When the second verb in a pivotal sentence is negated for advice or prohibition, use 别 (not 不): 劝他别喝酒 (advise him not to drink), 叫她别担心 (tell her not to worry).

items.question: 这本书，我_____看过了。
items.translation: (This book, I have already read it.)
items.explanation: Chinese uses topic-comment structure (主题-评论, zhǔtí-pínglùn) where the topic comes first, followed by a comment about it. Here 这本书 (this book) is the TOPIC, and 我已经看过了 is the COMMENT. The topic is separated by a comma. 已经 (yǐjīng) means 'already.' This structure emphasizes what you're talking about before saying what about it — very natural in Chinese conversation.

items.question: 中文，我觉得_____很有意思。
items.explanation: This is a nested topic-comment: 中文(Topic 1) → 我觉得语法很有意思(Comment containing sub-topic). The specific aspect 语法 (yǔfǎ, grammar) narrows the discussion from the broad topic 中文. This 'topic narrowing' is extremely common: start broad, then specify. 有意思 (yǒu yìsi) means 'interesting.'

items.question: 这件事，你_____怎么看？
items.explanation: 这件事(Topic) + 你自己怎么看(Comment with question). 自己 (zìjǐ) means 'yourself' — emphasizing personal opinion. 怎么看 (zěnme kàn) means 'how do you see it/what's your view.' Topic-comment questions are very common in Chinese: state the topic, then ask about it. This is more natural than the English equivalent.

items.question: 水果，我最喜欢吃_____。
items.translation: (As for fruit, I like eating apples the most.)
items.explanation: 水果(Topic) + 我最喜欢吃苹果(Comment). The topic 水果 (shuǐguǒ, fruit) establishes the category, and the comment states a preference within it. 最 (zuì) means 'most' — creating a superlative. 苹果 (píngguǒ) means 'apple.' This pattern is extremely common for expressing preferences: Category + 我最喜欢 + specific item.

items.question: 这个地方，_____很方便。
items.translation: (This place, the transportation is very convenient.)
items.explanation: 这个地方(Topic) + 交通很方便(Comment). The comment describes a specific aspect of the topic. 交通 (jiāotōng) means 'transportation/traffic.' 方便 (fāngbiàn) means 'convenient.' In Chinese, you don't need 'its' or possessive markers — the relationship between topic and comment is understood from context. This makes topic-comment more concise than English equivalents.

items.question: 那部电影，_____都说很好看。
items.explanation: 那部电影(Topic) + 大家都说很好看(Comment). 大家 (dàjiā) means 'everyone.' 都 (dōu) means 'all.' 好看 (hǎokàn) means 'good-looking/great to watch.' Notice there's no pronoun 'it' in the comment — Chinese doesn't need to repeat the topic as a pronoun. The topic is understood throughout the comment. This zero-anaphora is a key feature of Chinese.

items.question: 今天的天气，_____还是挺热的。
items.explanation: 今天的天气(Topic) + 虽然下雨了还是挺热的(Complex comment with concession). 虽然...还是 (suīrán...háishi) means 'although...still.' This shows that the comment can be a complex sentence. 挺 (tǐng) means 'quite/rather' in spoken Chinese. The topic-comment structure can handle sophisticated arguments while keeping the main subject clear at the start.

items.question: 他这个人，做事_____认真。
items.translation: (Him as a person, he does things very seriously.)
items.explanation: 他这个人(Topic - describing 'him as a person') + 做事非常认真(Comment). 非常 (fēicháng) means 'very/extremely.' 认真 (rènzhēn) means 'serious/conscientious.' The phrase 他这个人 is a topic-marker idiom meaning 'as for him/speaking of him.' It's used when evaluating someone's character. This pattern is extremely common in Chinese when describing people.

items.question: 昨天的考试，你_____了吗？
items.explanation: 昨天的考试(Topic) + 你考过了吗(Comment with question). 考过 (kǎoguò) means 'passed the exam' — 过 here is a result complement meaning 'successfully.' The question particle 吗 goes at the end. Topic-comment is the default way to bring up a subject in Chinese conversation — much more natural than saying 'Did you pass yesterday's exam?'

items.question: 这种菜，我_____。
items.translation: (This type of vegetable, I haven't eaten it before.)
items.explanation: 这种菜(Topic) + 我没吃过(Comment). 没吃过 (méi chīguò) means 'have never eaten' — 没 negates the experience marker 过. The topic 这种菜 (zhè zhǒng cài) uses the measure word 种 (zhǒng, kind/type). Again, no pronoun 'it' is needed after the comment subject 我. Chinese relies on the topic to carry meaning through the sentence.

items.question: 明天的会议，_____几点开始？
items.translation: (Tomorrow's meeting, what time does it start?)
items.explanation: 明天的会议(Topic) + 你知道几点开始吗(Comment asking for information). 你知道 (nǐ zhīdào) means 'do you know.' 几点 (jǐ diǎn) means 'what time.' This double-layer comment (you know + what time it starts) shows how flexible topic-comment can be. The topic sets the context, and the comment can contain embedded questions.

items.question: 北京的冬天，_____特别冷。
items.translation: (Beijing's winter, the wind is especially cold.)
items.explanation: 北京的冬天(Topic) + 风特别冷(Comment about a specific aspect). 风 (fēng) means 'wind.' 特别 (tèbié) means 'especially.' This topic-narrowing pattern is very productive: Location's Season(Topic) + Specific Feature + Description. No possessive is needed — the relationship is understood. Compare: 'Beijing's winter, the wind is especially cold' vs the Chinese's more natural 'Beijing's winter — wind especially cold.'

items.question: 那个学生，成绩_____，但是人很好。
items.translation: (That student, grades aren't great, but they're a nice person.)
items.explanation: 那个学生(Topic) + 成绩不太好，但是人很好(Complex comment with contrast). 不太好 (bú tài hǎo) means 'not very good.' 但是 (dànshì) means 'but.' Topic-comment easily handles contrasting descriptions: set the topic, then present multiple aspects. This is more natural in Chinese than saying 'That student's grades aren't good but they are nice.'

items.question: 我们公司，_____最多。
items.translation: (Our company, there are the most young people.)
items.explanation: 我们公司(Topic) + 年轻人最多(Comment). 年轻人 (niánqīngrén) means 'young people.' 最多 (zuì duō) means 'the most.' No verb 'to have' is needed — the topic-comment structure implies existence. This is much more concise than English: rather than 'In our company, there are the most young people,' Chinese simply states 'Our company — young people most many.'

items.question: 这家餐厅，_____我推荐鱼。
items.translation: (This restaurant, for dishes I recommend the fish.)
items.explanation: 这家餐厅(Broad Topic) + 菜的话(Narrowed Topic) + 我推荐鱼(Comment). 的话 (dehuà) means 'if we're talking about/as for' — it marks a conditional or narrowing topic. 推荐 (tuījiàn) means 'to recommend.' This double-topic pattern (broad → narrow → comment) is very natural and elegant in Chinese, allowing speakers to guide the conversation from general to specific.

items.question: 他是昨天_____的。
items.explanation: The 是...的 (shì...de) construction emphasizes WHEN, WHERE, HOW, or WHO of a past action. Here 是昨天(when) + 来(action) + 的 emphasizes that the arrival happened YESTERDAY, not some other time. Without 是...的, 他昨天来了 simply states a fact. With 是...的, the focus shifts to the time. This is one of the most important intermediate Chinese structures.

items.question: 我是坐飞机_____的。
items.explanation: 是坐飞机(how/by what means) + 来(action) + 的. This emphasizes HOW the speaker traveled — by plane, not by train or car. 坐飞机 (zuò fēijī) means 'to take a plane.' The 是...的 pattern is commonly used to answer 'how did you get here?' type questions. The emphasis falls on whatever comes between 是 and the verb.

items.question: 这本书是在哪儿_____的？
items.translation: (Where did you buy this book? [emphasis on 'where'])
items.explanation: 是在哪儿(where) + 买(action) + 的 emphasizes the LOCATION of the purchase. 在哪儿 (zài nǎr) means 'where/at what place.' When asking about details of a known past event (you know they bought a book, but WHERE?), 是...的 is the natural choice. Compare: 你在哪儿买了这本书？ (neutral) vs 这本书是在哪儿买的？ (focused on location).

items.question: 他们是去年_____的。
items.translation: (They got married last year. [emphasis on 'last year'])
items.explanation: 是去年(when) + 结婚(action) + 的 emphasizes WHEN they married. 结婚 (jiéhūn) means 'to get married.' For major life events that are already known, Chinese speakers use 是...的 to discuss specific details: when exactly, where, how. This is natural gossip/conversation Chinese — 'They got married LAST YEAR' (not this year).

items.question: 你是_____来中国的？
items.translation: (When did you come to China? [emphasis on 'when'])
items.explanation: 是 + 什么时候(when) + 来中国 + 的. Question words fit naturally into the 是...的 structure. 什么时候 (shénme shíhou) means 'when.' Common question patterns: 是什么时候...的 (when), 是在哪儿...的 (where), 是怎么...的 (how), 是跟谁...的 (with whom). The question word replaces the emphasized element.

items.question: 我是跟朋友_____去的。
items.translation: (I went together with a friend. [emphasis on 'with a friend'])
items.explanation: 是跟朋友一起(with whom) + 去(action) + 的 emphasizes WHO accompanied the speaker. 跟 (gēn) means 'with.' 一起 (yīqǐ) means 'together.' Note: 的 can come after the verb (去的) or after the object if there is one (去北京的). Both positions are correct, but 去的 (verb + 的) is more common in spoken Chinese.

items.question: 这个蛋糕是妈妈_____的。
items.translation: (This cake was made by mom. [emphasis on 'mom'])
items.explanation: 是妈妈(who) + 做(action) + 的 emphasizes WHO made the cake. This highlights the agent — it was MOM who made it, not anyone else. The 是...的 construction can emphasize the doer of the action in a way similar to English cleft sentences: 'It was mom who made it.' This is very useful for correcting misunderstandings or clarifying responsibility.

items.question: 她不是在北京_____的。
items.translation: (She wasn't born in Beijing. [emphasis: NOT in Beijing])
items.explanation: 不是在北京(negative emphasis on where) + 出生(action) + 的. To negate 是...的, place 不 before 是: 不是...的. 出生 (chūshēng) means 'to be born.' This negates the emphasized detail: she WAS born (known fact), but NOT in Beijing (correcting the location). Compare: 她没出生在北京 (neutral) vs 她不是在北京出生的 (emphatic correction).

items.question: 你是为什么_____学中文的？
items.translation: (Why did you decide to study Chinese? [emphasis on 'why'])
items.explanation: 是为什么(why) + 开始(action) + 学中文 + 的 emphasizes the REASON for starting to study Chinese. 为什么 (wèishéme) means 'why.' 开始 (kāishǐ) means 'to begin/start.' The 是...的 structure is perfect for 'getting the story' — asking about motivations and circumstances behind known actions. Very common in interviews and conversations.

items.question: 我们是在大学_____的。
items.explanation: 是在大学(where) + 认识(action) + 的 emphasizes WHERE they met. 认识 (rènshi) means 'to meet/know someone.' This is the classic way to tell your 'how we met' story. The 是...的 naturally fits narratives about relationships: 我们是在大学认识的 (met at uni), 是朋友介绍的 (through a friend), 是2020年结婚的 (married in 2020).

items.question: 这件衣服是在网上_____的。
items.explanation: 是在网上(where/how) + 买(action) + 的 emphasizes that the purchase was made ONLINE. 网上 (wǎngshàng) means 'online/on the internet.' Notice that 在网上 can indicate both location (online space) and method (online shopping). This pattern is very common in modern Chinese for discussing purchases, bookings, and digital interactions.

items.question: 他是自己_____来的，不是别人送的。
items.translation: (He drove here himself, nobody gave him a ride.)
items.explanation: 是自己开车(how, by whom) + 来的 emphasizes that he DROVE HIMSELF. 自己 (zìjǐ) means 'oneself.' 开车 (kāichē) means 'to drive.' The second clause 不是别人送的 (not dropped off by someone else) uses the negative 是...的 to contrast. Pairing affirmative and negative 是...的 creates clear emphasis: 'It WAS X, NOT Y.'

items.question: 你的中文是_____学的？
items.explanation: 是在哪儿(where) + 学(action) + 的 asks about the LOCATION of language learning. All four options are valid 是...的 question words, but 在哪儿 (zài nǎr, where) is correct here. Native speakers frequently ask foreigners 你的中文是在哪儿学的 — it's the most natural way to express curiosity about someone's Chinese learning background.

items.question: 那封信是用中文_____的。
items.translation: (That letter was written in Chinese. [emphasis on 'in Chinese'])
items.explanation: 是用中文(with what/in what language) + 写(action) + 的 emphasizes the MEDIUM — Chinese, not English or another language. 用 (yòng) means 'using/with.' 写 (xiě) means 'to write.' The 是...的 structure works perfectly with 用 to emphasize instruments, tools, languages, or methods: 是用筷子吃的 (ate with chopsticks), 是用手机拍的 (took photos with phone).

items.question: 这个项目是_____完成的？
items.explanation: 是怎么(how) + 完成(action) + 的 asks about the METHOD of completion. 怎么 (zěnme) means 'how.' 完成 (wánchéng) means 'to complete.' This is essential for asking about processes and methods of already-completed actions. In business and academic contexts, 是怎么...的 is the go-to pattern: 是怎么解决的 (how was it solved), 是怎么做到的 (how was it achieved).

items.question: 我吃_____午饭了。
items.explanation: 了 (le) after a verb indicates COMPLETION of an action — this is verb-了 (动态助词). 吃了 means 'have eaten/ate.' There are two types of 了: (1) verb-了 after the verb = completed action, (2) sentence-了 at sentence end = change of state. Here 吃了 uses verb-了 to show the action is done. The second 了 at the end reinforces the current relevance.

items.question: 他来_____三天了。
items.translation: (He has been here for three days.)
items.explanation: Verb + 了 + duration + 了 indicates an action that started in the past and continues to the present. 来了三天了 means 'has been here for three days (and still is).' The first 了 marks the completion of 'arriving,' the sentence-final 了 indicates the situation continues. This double-了 pattern is critical for expressing ongoing durations in Chinese.

items.question: 下雨_____。
items.translation: (It's raining now. / It has started raining.)
items.explanation: Sentence-final 了 (语气助词) indicates a CHANGE OF STATE — something is now different from before. 下雨了 means 'It has started raining' (it wasn't raining before, now it is). This is NOT about completion but about a new situation. Compare: 下雨了 (it started raining = change) vs 下了雨 (it rained = completed). This distinction is one of the trickiest aspects of Chinese grammar.

items.question: 我买了_____本书。
items.explanation: When verb-了 is followed by an object, a QUANTITY word or modifier must appear before the object. 买了三本书 is correct. *买了书 sounds incomplete — Chinese requires specifying quantity after verb-了 with an object. You need: number + measure word, 一些, 很多, or similar. This is a key rule: V + 了 + quantity/descriptor + object.

items.question: 我不想吃_____。
items.explanation: 不想...了 indicates a CHANGE in desire — 'I no longer want to / I don't want to anymore.' The 了 here is sentence-final 了 marking change of state. Previously the speaker wanted to eat, now they don't. This pattern is extremely common: 不想去了 (don't want to go anymore), 不喜欢了 (don't like it anymore), 不行了 (it's not working anymore).

items.question: 他长_____了。
items.explanation: Adjective + 了 indicates a change — something has BECOME different. 长高了 (zhǎng gāo le) means 'has grown taller.' 长 (zhǎng) means 'to grow.' The 了 signals that taller is a NEW state. This pattern works with many adjectives: 变好了 (became better), 变冷了 (became cold), 胖了 (gained weight), 瘦了 (lost weight). The change meaning of 了 is fundamental.

items.question: 我学了两年_____中文了。
items.translation: (I have been studying Chinese for two years.)
items.explanation: 学了两年的中文了 — this shows duration with an object. The structure is: V + 了 + duration + 的 + object + 了. The 的 connects the duration to the object. Some speakers omit 的, saying 学了两年中文了, which is also acceptable in spoken Chinese. The double-了 (one after verb, one at sentence end) emphasizes that the studying continues to the present.

items.question: 别_____了！
items.explanation: 别 + V + 了 means 'stop doing something / don't do it anymore.' 别说了 (bié shuō le) means 'stop talking.' The 了 indicates the action should CHANGE — from happening to not happening. This is different from 别说 (don't speak — a general prohibition). 别说了 specifically means 'you've been talking, now stop.' Very common in daily Chinese: 别哭了 (stop crying), 别笑了 (stop laughing).

items.question: 我们走_____吧。
items.explanation: V + 了 + 吧 suggests starting a NEW action — 'let's do it now.' 走了吧 means 'let's go (it's time).' The 了 marks the transition from waiting to leaving. 吧 (ba) adds a suggestion tone. This combination is extremely common for prompting action: 吃了吧 (let's eat now), 开始了吧 (let's start), 回去了吧 (let's go back). The 了 signals 'now is the time.'

items.question: 他已经_____了。
items.explanation: 已经 (yǐjīng, already) + V + 了 emphasizes that an action is ALREADY completed. 已经走了 means 'has already left.' When 已经 combines with verb-了, it reinforces the completion. This is a very clear and common way to state that something is done: 已经吃了 (already ate), 已经做完了 (already finished doing), 已经到了 (already arrived).

items.question: 太贵_____，我不买。
items.translation: (It's too expensive, I won't buy it.)
items.explanation: 太 + adj + 了 is a fixed pattern meaning 'too (much).' 太贵了 (tài guì le) means 'too expensive.' Despite looking like change-of-state 了, this is actually a degree expression. 太...了 wraps around the adjective for emphasis: 太好了 (too good/great), 太难了 (too difficult), 太远了 (too far). This pattern is one of the first uses of 了 that learners encounter.

items.question: 快要下雨_____，我们回家吧。
items.translation: (It's about to rain, let's go home.)
items.explanation: 快要...了 (kuài yào...le) means 'about to / soon going to.' 快要下雨了 means 'it's about to rain.' The 了 here signals an IMMINENT change. Similar patterns: 要...了 (going to), 快...了 (almost), 就要...了 (about to). These all use sentence-final 了 to mark that a new situation is approaching. 快要毕业了 (about to graduate), 快要到了 (almost arrived).

items.question: 我吃了饭_____去上班。
items.translation: (After I eat, I'll go to work.)
items.explanation: V + 了... + 再 + V2 means 'after doing V1, then do V2.' 吃了饭再去 means 'eat first, then go.' Here 了 marks the completion of eating as a condition for the next action, and 再 (zài) means 'then/afterwards.' This sequential pattern is very useful: 看了再说 (let's discuss after seeing), 想了再决定 (decide after thinking). 再 emphasizes the sequence.

items.question: 你吃了_____没有？
items.explanation: V + 了 + O + 没有 is the affirmative-negative question form for completed actions. 你吃了饭没有 asks 'Have you eaten (or not)?' This is equivalent to 你吃饭了吗 but uses a different question structure. Answer positively: 吃了 (yes, eaten). Answer negatively: 还没吃 (not yet). Note: use 没/没有 (not 不) to negate completed actions with 了.

items.question: 我等了他半个_____。
items.translation: (I waited for him for half an hour.)
items.explanation: V + 了 + object + duration shows how long an action lasted on a completed occasion. 等了他半个小时 means 'waited for him for half an hour.' The verb-了 marks the waiting as complete. 半个小时 (bàn ge xiǎoshí) means 'half an hour.' When the object is a person, it comes before the duration: V了 + person + duration. When it's a thing: V了 + duration + 的 + thing.

items.question: 他站_____说话。
items.explanation: 着 (zhe) after a verb indicates a CONTINUING STATE or ongoing action. V1 + 着 + V2 means 'doing V2 while in the state of V1.' 站着说话 means 'speak while standing.' Unlike 了 (completion) or 过 (experience), 着 emphasizes that the action is SUSTAINED. The first verb describes a maintained posture or state, and the second verb is the main action happening simultaneously.

items.question: 门_____着呢。
items.explanation: V + 着 + 呢 describes a current STATE resulting from an action. 门开着呢 means 'the door is (in the state of being) open.' 呢 (ne) reinforces the ongoing nature. 开着 doesn't mean 'opening' (action in progress) but rather 'is open' (resulting state). This is crucial: 着 can indicate either ongoing action OR resulting state. Common examples: 灯亮着 (light is on), 窗户关着 (window is closed).

items.question: 她穿_____一件红色的裙子。
items.explanation: 穿着 (chuānzhe) means 'is wearing' — describing the STATE of having clothes on. V + 着 is used for describing what someone is currently wearing, carrying, or holding. 一件 (yī jiàn) is the measure word for clothing. 裙子 (qúnzi) means 'dress/skirt.' Compare: 她穿了一件裙子 (she put on a dress — completed action) vs 她穿着一件裙子 (she is wearing a dress — ongoing state).

items.question: 墙上挂_____一幅画。
items.translation: (A painting is hanging on the wall.)
items.explanation: Location + V + 着 + Object describes something existing in a state at a location. 墙上挂着一幅画 means 'on the wall hangs a painting.' 挂 (guà) means 'to hang.' This pattern is very common for describing scenes and environments: 桌上放着一本书 (a book is placed on the table), 门口站着一个人 (someone is standing at the door). The 着 creates a static, descriptive image.

items.question: 他躺_____看书。
items.explanation: V1 + 着 + V2 means 'do V2 while maintaining V1 state.' 躺着看书 (tǎngzhe kànshū) means 'read while lying down.' 躺 means 'to lie down.' The 着 keeps the posture verb active while the second action proceeds. Common combinations: 坐着吃 (eat while sitting), 走着聊 (chat while walking), 站着等 (wait while standing). The posture verb always comes first.

items.question: 请_____着点儿。
items.explanation: V + 着 + 点儿 is a softened request to maintain an action briefly. 等着点儿 means 'wait a bit.' 点儿 (diǎnr) adds a casual, slightly diminished tone. The 着 emphasizes continuity — keep waiting. This pattern is colloquial and common: 拿着 (hold this), 记着 (remember/keep in mind), 看着 (keep watching). It's a gentle command to maintain a state.

items.question: 外面下_____雨呢。
items.explanation: V + 着 + 呢 for weather describes an ongoing atmospheric condition. 下着雨呢 means 'it's raining (right now, continuously).' Compare three options: 下雨了 (it started raining — change), 下着雨呢 (it's raining — ongoing), 下过雨 (it has rained — experience). 着 is the right choice when emphasizing that the rain is CURRENTLY happening and continuing. 呢 reinforces the present-tense ongoing feel.

items.question: 她笑_____回答了我的问题。
items.translation: (She answered my question with a smile.)
items.explanation: V1 + 着 + V2 can describe manner — HOW the second action is performed. 笑着回答 means 'answered while smiling/with a smile.' The 着 turns the first verb into a manner description for the main action. This creates vivid, descriptive sentences: 哭着说 (said while crying), 跑着过来 (came over running), 唱着跳舞 (dancing while singing). Very common in storytelling and descriptions.

items.question: 他手里拿_____一把伞。
items.translation: (He is holding an umbrella in his hand.)
items.explanation: Body part + V + 着 + object describes what someone is holding/carrying. 手里拿着一把伞 means 'holding an umbrella in hand.' 拿 (ná) means 'to hold/take.' 一把 (yī bǎ) is the measure word for umbrellas. This is a set pattern for describing people's appearance: 背上背着包 (carrying a backpack), 头上戴着帽子 (wearing a hat), 脚上穿着鞋 (wearing shoes on feet).

items.question: 别_____着我！
items.explanation: 别 + V + 着 means 'don't keep doing' or 'stop maintaining that state.' 别看着我 means 'stop looking at me / don't stare.' The 着 emphasizes the CONTINUOUS nature of the unwanted action. Compare: 别看 (don't look — general) vs 别看着 (don't keep looking/stare — ongoing). 别 + V + 着 is more emphatic and implies the action is already happening and should stop.

items.question: 教室里坐_____很多学生。
items.translation: (Many students are sitting in the classroom.)
items.explanation: Place + V + 着 + number/quantity + people describes a scene. 教室里坐着很多学生 means 'in the classroom sit many students.' This is a DESCRIPTIVE existential sentence — it paints a picture of what the scene looks like. The 着 creates a snapshot of the moment. This pattern is fundamental for Chinese description: 路上走着很多人 (many people walking on the road), 操场上跑着几个孩子 (a few kids running on the playground).

items.question: 你听_____，外面有声音。
items.explanation: V + 着 as a command means 'keep doing/pay attention.' 听着 means 'listen (and keep listening).' This imperative use of 着 is direct and sometimes forceful. It tells someone to maintain attention or action: 看着 (watch carefully), 记着 (remember this), 拿着 (hold this). In urgent or serious situations, V + 着 commands are very common and convey importance.

items.question: 孩子们_____着眼睛听故事。
items.translation: (The children listen to the story with wide eyes.)
items.explanation: 睁着眼睛 (zhēngzhe yǎnjing) means 'with eyes open wide.' 睁 means 'to open (eyes).' Again, V1 + 着 describes the manner/state during V2 (听故事, listening to a story). This creates a vivid image of attentive children. Body-state descriptions with 着 are very expressive: 闭着眼睛 (with eyes closed), 张着嘴 (with mouth open), 皱着眉头 (with furrowed brows).

items.question: 这个问题我一直_____着呢。
items.translation: (I've been thinking about this problem all along.)
items.explanation: 一直 + V + 着 + 呢 means 'have been continuously doing.' 一直想着 means 'have been thinking about it all along.' 一直 (yīzhí) means 'all along/continuously.' The combination of 一直 + 着 + 呢 creates a triple emphasis on duration and continuity. This pattern shows dedication or persistence: 一直等着呢 (been waiting all along), 一直记着呢 (have always remembered).

items.question: 窗户_____着，请关上。
items.translation: (The window is open, please close it.)
items.explanation: 开着 describes the current STATE of the window (open), followed by a request to change it (请关上, please close).
